question: counterexample to: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.
expected_answer: Many people think it is a main good thing about thinking. One might feel like look at it as a very good idea of how far you know, not saying too much, and being ready to say when you are wrong. That way of looking shows a lot of what we mean by not being full of you when we think. But a story that shows this way of looking is not enough and can be made without big trouble. Imagine a person who works with numbers and is very well known who has a very good idea of the edge of what she can do. She knows exactly which parts of number work she can do and which are past what she has learned. She never gives thoughts on things she does not know, and when she makes a wrong thing she says it quickly and does not get angry. She is kind in giving thank for the work of the people she works with. All three parts of the idea seem to be true. But when you look at how she feels more close, you see that she looks at the edge of what she knows with a calm not caring. She does not see them as things that need fixing or as reasons to ask for help from people who know more; instead, she sees them as just facts about her, no more needing a reply than the color of her eyes. When a friend tries to tell her about the thing that happens in a close place, she says no, saying she does not care about things she does not already know. It seems, then, that a very good idea of what you know and not saying too much are not enough for not being full of you about thinking. What else is needed is a way to look at your own edge – a feeling that they are reasons to keep looking or to listen to people who know more than you.
english_query: counterexample to: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the case puts forward is a claim about what it means for a person to have a low ego about what they know. It says that a low‑ego person must have three things: first, the person must have a true look at how far their own know and brain skill can go; second, the person must not say they know more than their skill really lets them; and third, the person must be ready to say when they are wrong and to give credit when other people add something useful.

The second idea is a different case that is meant to show that the three things do not always go together. In this case a person can have a true look at the edge of their own know and brain skill, and can also not say they know more than their skill lets them, but still not be ready to say when they are wrong or to give credit to others. Yet other people still see this person as having a low ego about what they know because the person often listens and does not act like they are the only source of good ideas. This different case is used to say that the three‑thing description does not capture all the ways a low‑ego person can act, so the “if and only if” link in the first idea is wrong.

The third idea looks at whether the thinking in the case is good. The reasoning that a single different case can break a definition that uses “if and only if” is normally a strong move, because a single counter‑example does show that a definition that says “all and only those who have the three things are low‑ego” cannot be true. The thinking is good if the different case really does show a person who is low‑ego while missing the third thing. If the case does not truly show a low‑ego person, or if people would not call that person low‑ego without the third thing, then the reasoning would be weaker. Overall, the structure of the argument – stating a definition, giving a case that does not fit the definition but seems to fit the idea, and then saying the definition must be wrong – is a solid way to test a definition, so the thinking behind the case is generally good.

question: counterexample to: A judgement is synthetic a priori if and only if (i) the predicate is not contained in the concept of the subject, (ii) the judgement is known independently of experience, and (iii) the judgement's truth is grounded in the pure forms of intuition or the categories of the understanding.
expected_answer:
The ideas of the person who wrote about new things we have before we see them are seen as one of the most big and most talked about adds to how we know things in the long time of that thing.

Against both the person who says reason is the main way to know, who says that things we have before we see are always just about breaking things down, and the person who says seeing and feeling are the main way to know, who says that any real things we have before we see can not be, the person who wrote about these ideas says that there are thoughts that give new facts and can be known without any seeing or feeling.

The things he shows — 7 and 5 make 12, every thing that happens has a reason, and the simple ideas of flat shape work — have been talked about a lot since then.

The meaning we look at now shows the idea of the person who wrote about these ideas by putting the new things we have before we see on the simple ways the mind sees and the main ways the mind sorts.

One may see, however, that this putting is itself a real big idea about thinking, and a thing that many people do not all agree on.

A story that shows the not enough of this look comes up when we think about the view of the person who thinks seeing and feeling are the main way to know and who does not like the things of the big idea of deep thinking by the person who wrote about these ideas.

Such a person may say that some thoughts — every thing that comes after has a reason, for case — look like new things we have before we see, but will say that the before part is not shown by the simple ways the mind sees or the main ways the mind sorts.

She may give another reason using ideas about how the mind gets set, how we get things from our past, or how we need it to work.

Thing number three, which joins the new things we have before we see to a special big idea of deep real things by the person who wrote about these ideas, then makes the meaning take sides where it should be just.

A good story about the new things we have before we see, if those things are real, must fit with many different ways of looking at how we know, instead of thinking that one big idea about thinking is the only real one.
english_query: counterexample to: A judgement is synthetic a priori if and only if (i) the predicate is not contained in the concept of the subject, (ii) the judgement is known independently of experience, and (iii) the judgement's truth is grounded in the pure forms of intuition or the categories of the understanding.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward is a way to tell when a statement belongs to a special kind of knowledge. The claim is that such a statement must have three parts. First, the part we say about the thing must not already be inside the idea of the thing itself. Second, we must be able to know the statement without any need to see or feel anything in the world. Third, the rightness of the statement must come from the simple ways our mind can see or sort things, not from any particular thing we have seen.  

The second idea is an example that the speaker says shows the three parts do not always line up the way the first idea says. They point to a statement that looks like it meets the three parts, but they argue that it is not really of the special kind the first idea talks about. For instance, they might talk about a statement about numbers that seems to be known without seeing any objects and whose saying part is not already in the idea of the numbers, yet they say its rightness comes from the way we count, which they think is not one of the simple ways the mind can see. Because the statement does not fit the label the first idea gives, they say it is a counter‑example.  

From this they think it follows that the three‑part rule cannot be a perfect way to pick out that special kind of knowledge. If one statement can meet the three parts and still not be of that kind, then the rule must be wrong or at least incomplete. So the conclusion they draw is that the original definition needs to be changed or dropped.  

Looking at the thinking, the move from the example to the conclusion is only as good as the claim that the example really does meet all three parts while still not being of the special kind. If the example actually slips on one of the three parts, or if the idea of “simple ways our mind can see” is understood differently, then the conclusion does not follow. The reasoning is clear in that it shows a possible mismatch, but the strength of the point depends on whether the example truly satisfies the three conditions as they are meant. If the example does satisfy them, the thinking is strong and shows a real problem with the rule. If the example does not satisfy them, the thinking is weak because it would be based on a mistaken claim about the example. In short, the argument is only good if the example really is what the speaker says it is.

question: counterexample to: A division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period is defined by a set of distinctive characteristics that are shared by the events and developments within it, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant transitions or ruptures, and (iii) the periodisation is applicable across the geographic region under study.
expected_answer:
One might say that a real way to cut time into parts shows each time by things that are the same and also things that are different, makes lines at big changes, and can be used for the whole area we look at. This picture shows that the parts fit together inside and can be used outside, like good ways to cut time into parts do.  

But one case that shows this not enough can come from the story of the west. The usual cut between the old time and the new time puts the line at about the year 1400. In the towns in the west, this line shows real changes: the new start of old school learning, the coming of new art ways, the growing of buying and selling and money making. This way to cut time into parts meets all three parts when used for the towns in the west.  

But in the north lands, the ways people live, the ways money works, and the ways they make art that belong to the old time kept going far into the year 1500. The line at year 1400, even if it shows a big change in one area, tells a wrong story about how things change in another area.  

One can see that the idea, by saying it only has to work for the whole area and not saying it must fit the different times of change in each part, lets a plan made for one place be used on places where it does not fit the time.
english_query: counterexample to: A division of history into periods is legitimate if and only if (i) each period is defined by a set of distinctive characteristics that are shared by the events and developments within it, (ii) the boundaries between periods correspond to significant transitions or ruptures, and (iii) the periodisation is applicable across the geographic region under study.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is put forward says that cutting up the story of the past into parts can be called right only when three things are true. The first thing is that each part has a set of things that are the same for the things that happen inside that part. The second thing is that the lines that separate the parts line up with big changes or breaks in the way things happen. The third thing is that the way we cut up the past works for the whole area that we are looking at, not just for one small place.

The second idea gives an example that they say shows the opposite. They talk about a part of the past that they call the “Industrial time” in Europe. They say that this part has the same set of things – new machines, factories, and city growth – for all the things that happen inside it. They also say that the line that starts this part is the moment when the first big factories were built, which is a big break from the way things were before. Finally they say that this way of cutting up the past works for the whole area of Europe because most of the countries there saw the same new machines and factories. Yet they point out that in many other places, such as large parts of Asia, the same “Industrial time” does not show the same set of things and the line does not line up with a big break there. So they claim that even though the three things seem to be met in Europe, the way of cutting up the past still does not work for the whole area that was meant to be covered.

The third idea that follows from this example is that the claim with the three things cannot be true in general. Because they have found one case that meets the three things in one place but does not work for other places, they say the whole rule that the three things are enough must be wrong.

The fourth idea looks at whether the thinking is good. The logic that one example that goes against the rule shows the rule is false is a correct kind of thinking – a single opposite case can break a rule that says “all cases must have these three things”. However, the strength of the argument depends on whether the example truly has all three things as described. If the “Industrial time” in Europe does not really have the same set of things for every country, or if the line does not truly match a big break for all of Europe, then the example does not fully meet the three conditions and the argument is weaker. If the example does meet the three conditions in Europe but still fails for other places, then the reasoning is strong and shows that the rule is not enough to make a good cut‑up of the past for the whole world. In that case the thinking is good because it points out that a rule that works in one area does not automatically work everywhere.

question: counterexample to: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denial of P leads to a contradiction, and (iii) P is a priori knowable — that is, its truth can be established independently of experience.
expected_answer: one might say that a thing that have to be true is a thing that is true, that saying it is not true leads to something that can not be true, and that we can know it just by thinking. this talk has an old story: a group of people who think about reason from old person who thinks to people who think that only things we can see are real said that have to be true, things that are true just by how they are said, and things we can know just by thinking were all the same. a case that shows this thought is not enough can come from how we talk about words. think about the thought that the star we see in the sky after the day is the same star we see in the sky early in the day — that the bright thing we see in the sky after the day is the very same thing we see in the sky early in the day. this thought is true. also, because both names point to the same thing, saying the thought is not true would be saying that the thing is not the thing, which can not be true. so the thought meets the first two things. but it does not meet the thing after the second: finding out that the star we see after the day and the star we see early in the day are the same thing needed looking at the sky with things we use. no lot of just thinking could have shown they are the same. it seems that the talk that says we have to be able to know something just by thinking, as a have to be true thing, can not fit ideas that are have to be true because the two names point to the same thing, not because of the way the thought is said.
english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the denial of P leads to a contradiction, and (iii) P is a priori knowable — that is, its truth can be established independently of experience.
ugf_response:
The first idea in the case is a claim about what makes a statement always true. It says that a statement is always true exactly when three things happen: the statement is true, saying that the statement is not true creates a problem that cannot be true, and we can know that the statement is true without using what we see. The claim puts these three parts together and says that they are both needed and enough for a statement to be always true.

The second idea is an example that tries to show the claim is wrong. The example picks a statement that is true, but where saying it is not does not lead to a problem that cannot be true, and where we cannot know it without using what we see. For instance, a statement about what the weather is right now can be true, but saying it is not does not cause a logical problem, and we can only know if it is true by looking at the sky. Because this statement meets the first part of the claim but not the other two, yet it is not an always‑true statement, the example is meant to show that the three parts do not guarantee an always‑true statement.

The thinking that follows from the two ideas is that the original claim about always‑true statements is not reliable. The reasoning is good in the sense that it points out a clear mismatch: a statement can be true without fitting the other two conditions, so the three conditions cannot be the exact test for always‑true statements. However, the evaluation also depends on how we understand “a problem that cannot be true” and “knowing without using what we see.” If those phrases are taken in a stricter way, the example might not break the claim. Still, the overall move – to find a case where the three parts do not line up with an always‑true statement – is a solid way to show the original claim has a flaw.

question: counterexample to: A region has a particular climate if and only if (i) the average temperature and precipitation are stable over a long period, (ii) the region can be assigned to a recognised climatic zone, and (iii) the climatic conditions are determined by the region's latitude and altitude.
expected_answer:
A thought about the air and rain says that a place has some air and rain only when most hot or cold how it is and rain or snow stay the same for a long time, the place can be put in a called area, and the air and rain are set by how far north or south the place is and how high the place is.  

This thought works very well for many places of the world: the warm and wet parts near the middle line of the earth are warm and wet because they are near the middle line, and the top of high land are cold because they are high.  

But the thought meets a big hard with places that the air and rain that many places have is different from what we think for how far north or south they are and how high they are.  

Think about the north west side of a land. A city there is at about the same north or south how it is as the south end of a far north land, but its cold times are much less cold.  

The reason is not how far north or how high the place is but a warm water moving in a big water, which brings warm water from a big water on the east side to the north west side of the land and makes the hot or cold how it is more high than we would think.  

The next thought does not work: the place air and rain are not set by how far north or how high it is but by the change made by a water moving.  

But the north west side of the land in an easy way has the air and rain that many places have, and a well named one at that.  

The thought has made the reason of air and rain too easy by making it down to two things about where a place is, when in true life the things that set the place air and rain have water moving, the moving air that usually blows, and how close the place is to big water areas.
english_query: counterexample to: A region has a particular climate if and only if (i) the average temperature and precipitation are stable over a long period, (ii) the region can be assigned to a recognised climatic zone, and (iii) the climatic conditions are determined by the region's latitude and altitude.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person puts forward is that there is a place that has a certain kind of weather even though it does not meet any of the three things that were said to be needed for that kind of weather. They say that the place’s average heat and rain do not stay the same for a long time, that the place cannot be put into any known weather group, and that the place’s weather is not set by its north‑south position or its height above sea level. The second idea they draw from this is that because such a place exists, the statement that a place has a particular kind of weather only when all three of those things are true must be wrong. In other words, they think the existence of the place shows that the “if and only if” claim does not hold. The third idea that follows in their mind is that we should not use those three things as the only way to decide what kind of weather a place has.

Looking at the thinking, the move from the first idea to the second is a common way to test a claim: if you can find a case where the claim says something should be true but it is not, then the claim is false. That part of the reasoning is sound, as long as the place really does have the kind of weather they say it has. If the place’s weather does not actually fit the description, then the example does not break the claim and the reasoning falls apart. The step from the second idea to the third idea is also logical: once a claim is shown to be false, it cannot be used as a rule. However, the overall quality of the thinking depends on whether the example truly has the weather in question while lacking the three listed features. If the example meets at least one of the three features, or if its weather is not really the kind being talked about, then the reasoning is weak. If the example does meet all the conditions of the counter‑case, then the thinking is good and the original “if and only if” statement should be changed.

question: counterexample to: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) F is Archimedean — that is, for every element x of F there exists a natural number n such that n > x, (ii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield, and (iii) every element of F can be expressed as a ratio of two integers.
expected_answer:
One might think that a group of numbers that you can add and see which is bigger in order is whole if and only if it has the idea that you can always find a whole number bigger than any number, it has all the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number inside it, and every number in the group can be written as one whole number over another whole number.  

The first two ideas make sense: the real numbers have the idea that you can always find a whole number bigger than any number, and they do have all the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number inside them.  

The third idea might seem to make sense if what you know about these groups of numbers is only the ones that can be written as one whole number over another whole number. Every such number is, by the words that say what it is, a part of one whole number over another whole number, and that group of numbers follows all three ideas.  

Yet one must think about the real numbers – the group that most people think of as the main whole group of numbers. The real numbers have the idea that you can always find a whole number bigger than any number: for any number there is a whole number that is bigger. They have all the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number.  

But not every real number is a part of one whole number over another whole number. The number that when you put it together with itself gives the number 2 is not a part of whole numbers, a fact that people have shown a long time ago and is one of the first and biggest things people have found in numbers. It cannot be written as one whole number over another whole number, no matter how in a good way you try to pick the top and bottom whole numbers.  

The third idea of the words that say what it is does not work for the real numbers.  

But the real numbers are whole; in fact, having the idea that any group of real numbers that has at least one number and has a biggest number also has a smallest biggest number inside the group is what makes them whole.  

The words that say what the numbers are only talked about the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number, not about the idea that makes a group whole, and so they put together groups that do not have the idea with groups that do.
english_query: counterexample to: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) F is Archimedean — that is, for every element x of F there exists a natural number n such that n > x, (ii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield, and (iii) every element of F can be expressed as a ratio of two integers.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is put forward is a claim that there is a set of numbers that has three particular features. The first feature is that for any element in the set you can find a counting number that is bigger than it. The second feature is that the set includes every fraction made from two whole numbers, and it even includes all the fractions that we normally call rational numbers. The third feature is that every element of the set can be written as one whole number divided by another whole number. The person says that this set also has the property that there are no missing points – that is, it is full in the sense that every point that should be there is there.

From these three features, the second idea the person draws is that the usual statement “a set of numbers is full if and only if it has those three features” must be wrong. In other words, because this particular set has all three features but still is not full, the “if and only if” claim cannot be true. The person is using the example as a way to show that the rule does not work in every case.

To see whether the thinking is good, we look at the link between the three features and the idea of being full. The reasoning works only if the example truly has all three features and still is not full. If the example does indeed have a counting number larger than every element, contains all fractions, and lets every element be written as one whole number over another, then it does break the “if and only if” claim and the thinking is correct. However, if any part of the example fails – for instance, if the set does not really contain every fraction or if it actually is full when examined more closely – then the conclusion does not follow and the reasoning would be faulty. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example really meets all three stated conditions while lacking the full‑ness property. If it does, the argument is solid; if it does not, the argument falls apart.

question: counterexample to: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the speaker can use W to communicate with others, and (iii) W occurs in a natural language that is spoken by a community.
expected_answer: One might say that a word has meaning when it has a picture in the mind, can be used to talk, and is part of the words that many people use. This picture thought has been around a long time, and it says that good words are joined to something in the mind. But a case that shows this thought is not enough can be taken from the words we use about right and good in people life. Think about the word we call just. A person who talks uses this word with clear feeling, talks a lot about that thought, and talks well about things of the same way people act with each other, the thought that people get what they have done, the thought that people have what they are allowed to have, and the thought that people must do what they should. The word is part of the words that many many people use. All three parts are there. But it is not clear that the person who talks has any set picture in the mind for that thought. What would that picture look like? Maybe a person who can not see because something covers their eyes holding two sides, but that is a picture that many people use not a picture of what the thought really is. The meaning of that thought is not in any picture in the mind but in the word many other thoughts – the same way people act with each other, the thought that people get what they have done, the thought that people have what they are allowed to have, the thought that people must do what they should – and in the way it is used right in talk among many people about right and good. It seems that the thing that says a word must have a picture to be a good word leaves out or gets the meaning of thoughts that are not about pictures but about how they work in talk.
english_query: counterexample to: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the speaker can use W to communicate with others, and (iii) W occurs in a natural language that is spoken by a community.
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward says that a word has meaning only when three things happen. First, the word must be tied to a picture in the mind of the person who says it. Second, that person must be able to use the word to talk with other people. Third, the word must be part of a normal spoken language that a group of people use. The second idea they give is an example that they say shows the three things are not all needed. They tell about a sound that a small child makes to ask for milk. The child has a clear picture of milk in the mind, can use the sound to tell the mother what is wanted, but the sound is not a word that belongs to any normal spoken language used by a group. They say that because this sound still works as a word for the child, the three‑thing rule does not hold. The third idea is their judgment that the rule is therefore false. They think that finding even one case where a word works without meeting one of the three points is enough to knock the whole rule down.

Looking at what they say is true, the example of the child’s special sound is indeed a real situation where a “word” can have meaning without being part of a larger spoken group. The picture in the child’s mind and the ability to use the sound to talk with the mother are both there, while the third point about belonging to a group language is missing. So the factual part of their case is correct. 

What they think follows from that fact is that the three‑thing rule cannot be right. This step follows the normal way we check a rule that says “all cases must have these three parts.” If we can point to even one case that does not have all three parts but still works, the rule cannot be true for every case. That line of thinking is a good one, because a single counter‑example is enough to show a universal claim is false. 

Therefore, the breakdown is: the first idea is the rule itself, the second idea is the real example that breaks the rule, and the third idea is the conclusion that the rule is false. The example they give is true, the jump from the example to the conclusion is a correct move, and the overall thinking is solid.

question: counterexample to: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X cannot be partitioned into two non-empty disjoint open sets, (ii) any two points in X can be joined by a continuous path lying entirely within X, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the integers is constant.
expected_answer:
The places that most people see first when they start to look at shapes have a thing that can make a person think it is a must for being joined together. The closed short line between two numbers, the open flat round shape, and the ball shape are all joined together in the way we think about places, and in each case any two things can be joined by a way that does not break and stays inside the place. Every thing that does not break and goes from these places to whole numbers always gives the same whole number. These notes ask for a big talk: a place is joined together when you can not break it into two parts that both have at least one thing and do not share any things and are open, when any two things can be joined by a way that does not break, and when every thing that does not break and goes to whole numbers always gives the same whole number.  

But a story that shows this idea is not enough can be taken from the look at a part of a flat big place that acts in a strange way near its edge. Look at the whole thing that has the picture of a line that goes up and down a lot when the first thing gets close to the number that is nothing for the first thing in the part between the number that is nothing and one. This place has a line that goes up and down a lot together with an up and down line at the first thing is the number that is nothing. You can not break it into two parts that both have at least one thing and do not share any things and are open; it is joined together. Every thing that does not break and goes from it to whole numbers always gives the same whole number. The first idea and the third idea are true. But no way that does not break can join any thing on the up and down line to any thing on the line that goes up and down a lot, because such a way would have to go through many up and down moves in a short time. The second idea is not true. What the earlier stories have the same and why we added the way idea is that short lines between numbers, flat round shapes, and ball shapes are not just joined together but can be joined by a way that does not break. The talk, by making the way idea a must, puts together two different ideas about places and leaves out places that are really joined together in the way the first and third ideas say.
english_query: counterexample to: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X cannot be partitioned into two non-empty disjoint open sets, (ii) any two points in X can be joined by a continuous path lying entirely within X, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the integers is constant.
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is that there is a place we call Y that meets three simple facts. The first fact is that Y cannot be split into two parts that both have at least one point, that both look open, and that do not share any point. The second fact is that any two points in Y can be linked by a line that does not break and that stays completely inside Y. The third fact is that any rule that takes each point in Y and gives a whole number gives the same whole number for every point. They say all three of these facts are true for Y.

The second idea they draw from those facts is that, because the three facts are true, Y should be a single whole piece, that is, it should not be able to be broken into separate pieces. question: counterexample to: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circumstances, (ii) P's truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, without knowledge of the truth-values of its components, and (iii) P is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives.

question: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that they would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.
expected_answer: One might say that breaking a promise works like this: a person has broken a promise if and only if the person said words that meant he would do a certain thing, the other person trusted that promise, and the first person did not do the thing. The idea added a part – trust – that comes from the law of promises rather than from the idea of right and wrong about promises, where the need is on the act of giving a word and is broken by not doing the act alone.

question: counterexample to: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function f from A to B, (ii) f is injective, and (iii) f is surjective.
expected_answer:

By this idea, the two groups have the same size.  

Yet the numbers that are two times bigger are a part that is not the whole of the counting numbers: every number that is two times bigger is a counting number, but not every counting number is two times bigger. The idea then says the part has the same size as the whole – a thought that would be not right in a small case.  

One can see that what the small cases have in shared, and what the no end case breaks, is the idea that same size works together with the idea that a part is not as big as the whole. In other words, they think the rule does not work as a test for a good talk about a god. The third idea is an evaluation of the thinking. If the example really does have only things we can know by thinking and only sure steps, then it does show that having those two parts is not enough to guarantee the end point about a god with all good things, so the thinking that the rule is false is good. If, however, the example slips on either the starting things or the sure steps, then the example does not really break the rule and the claim that the rule is false would be weak. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example truly satisfies the first two parts; if it does, the reasoning is solid, and if it does not, the reasoning is not convincing.

question: counterexample to: A narrative N constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) N recounts events in their correct temporal sequence, (ii) N is grounded in authentic primary sources, and (iii) N provides a coherent account of why events occurred as they did.
expected_answer:
The most long past stories share a part that needs close look. When a person who wrote a long time ago told the fall of the big city that was the main place of an old group, he added the points of look of people who ran the city, outside war people, and people who thought about the one many people think about, showing how their different thoughts made the way things went. When a person who wrote a long time ago told the wars between the group from the east and the group from the west, he wrote down the points of look of both groups, making his story more full by showing that the same things meant different meaning for different people. When a person who wrote a long time ago told the big change in a far away land, he gave a say to country workers and city poor together with the people who led the group, showing that the meaning of the change came from the point of look of the one who lived it.

One could try to make a way to say what a good past story is by saying a good past story is one that tells things in the right order of time, uses real first hand things, and gives a clear why and how story. But a case that shows this way is not enough can be taken from the past writing about the church war. Think of a story made only from west church people – letters from the head of the church, notes from people who lived in a church house, papers from groups that fought in the war. It tells things in the right order. Its things are very real. It gives a clear why the church war started and how it went. All three parts are met. But the story leaves out all the points of look of the people who believed in a different one many people think about whose lands were taken, the people who believed in a group of ideas about the one many people think about that were killed on the way, and the big land in the east whose wants were often put below those of the people from the west who fought in the war. Its clear view is got by a simple leaving out.

One could see that what big stories share, and what the way does not ask for, is the putting in many points of look – the idea that past things are lived and thought about in different ways by different people, and that a story that leaves these points out gets a wrong clear view.
english_query: counterexample to: A narrative N constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) N recounts events in their correct temporal sequence, (ii) N is grounded in authentic primary sources, and (iii) N provides a coherent account of why events occurred as they did.
ugf_response:
The first idea is the rule that a story about the past is called a good past story only when three things are true. The first of those things is that the story tells the things that happened in the right order of time. The second thing is that the story is built on real first places we get info from about the past. The third thing is that the story gives a clear explanation of why the things happened in the way they did and that explanation makes sense.

The second idea is an example that tries to show the rule does not work. In this example a story does tell the things in the right order, it does use real first places we get info from, and it does give a clear explanation that makes sense. Yet the person who gives the example says that most people still do not think the story is a good past story because the story leaves out a big part of how people felt or because the story pushes a certain view that many think is unfair. In this way the example is meant to show that even when the three things are met, the story can still be seen as not a good past story.

The third idea is the claim that because this example exists, the rule cannot be true. The reasoning goes: if a rule says that three things are enough to make a story a good past story, and we have a story that has those three things but still is not seen as a good past story, then the rule must be wrong. So the person thinks the rule is disproved by the example.

The fourth idea is an evaluation of that reasoning. To see if the thinking is good we need to check whether the example really does have all three things the rule talks about. If the story leaves out something that the rule counts as part of the real first places we get info from, or if the explanation does not really make sense to most people, then the example does not truly meet the three things and the rule is not yet disproved. If, however, the story truly does meet the three things and still is judged bad for other reasons, then the rule may indeed be missing something important, and the reasoning that the rule is false would be strong. So the quality of the thinking depends on how well the example fits the three conditions and on whether the rule was meant to cover the extra part that the example brings up. If the example is accurate, the thinking is good; if the example is not accurate, the thinking is weak.

question: counterexample to: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.
expected_answer: It is often thought that many of our ideas are not always in the head at a time but are like a way of being ready. The look here says that an idea is ready if and only if a person is ready to say yes to the right words when the ask is made in the right way, the ready feeling comes from some earlier thought or way, and the person would really say yes to the words in normal times. A story that shows this look is not enough can be made like this. Think about the words that no big animal is lighter than one small weight. It looks like almost any thinking grown up person would say yes to these words if you ask, and would do it with full truth. But it seems very hard that most people have ever thought about this same words, or that any earlier thought about big animals and small weights has happened in the mind before. The ready feeling to say yes does not come from an earlier thought about making ideas but from the person big idea of the world, which gives the right pick as a simple guess when the time needs it. The look however says that the ready feeling must come from some earlier thought, and this law is not met. So we end up saying that the idea of a ready idea goes past what the look can hold, because it has to have words that the person has never thought about but that follow easy from what the person already knows.
english_query: counterexample to: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward is a claim about what it means for a person to have a belief that is “ready to act.” The claim says that a belief is of that kind only when three things happen. First, the person must be ready to say yes to the idea when the question is asked in the right way. Second, that readiness must come from some earlier thought or some earlier way the person’s mind worked. Third, the person would, under the usual things that happen, say yes to the idea truly when they think about it in a calm way.

The second idea is an example that is meant to show that the three things do not always line up the way the claim says. Imagine a person who, when asked a certain question, says yes because they want to fit in with others, not because they truly think the idea is right. The person’s yes‑answer is ready when the question is asked, so the first part of the claim is met. The person’s readiness comes from a memory of a time they were praised for saying yes, so the second part of the claim is also met. But when the person sits alone later and thinks about the idea, they do not truly believe it and would not say yes if they were alone. In that quiet thinking the person would not say yes sincerely, so the third part of the claim is not met. Yet the person still acted as if they had the ready belief when the question was asked. This shows that the three parts of the claim do not always work together to pick out the kind of belief the claim wants to talk about.

The third idea is the judgment that the reasoning used to give the example is good. The reasoning first takes the three parts of the claim and then shows a situation where the first two parts are true while the third part is false, yet the person still behaves as the claim says a ready belief would. Because the example fits the claim’s own description of the first two parts and still leads to a different result, it shows that the claim does not capture all the ways a person can be ready to say yes. In other words, the reasoning correctly points out a gap in the original claim, and the example works as a clear illustration of that gap. Therefore the thinking that the claim is not correct because of this example is a good piece of thinking.

question: counterexample to: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point a if and only if (i) f is defined at a, (ii) for every sequence (x_n) converging to a, the sequence (f(x_n)) converges to f(a), and (iii) f maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of a.
expected_answer:
Think about three things that always go without big changes.  

The first thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives the number times itself. It goes without big changes at every point on the line of true numbers. It works everywhere; for every line of numbers that gets closer to a given point, the new line of numbers also gets closer to the number times itself of that point; and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that also stay not too big.  

The second thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives a number that gets bigger fast when the number that goes in gets bigger. It goes without big changes at every point. It works everywhere; it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to a point, the new line also gets closer; and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that also stay not too big.  

The third thing is the thing that takes a number that goes in and gives a number that goes up and down in the same way. It goes without big changes at every point. It works everywhere; it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to a point, the new line also gets closer; and it itself stays not too big, so it also turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big.  

From these cases we can make a thought: a thing that changes a number into another number is without big changes at a point if and only if it works there, it keeps the idea that when a line of numbers gets closer to that point the new line also gets closer, and it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big in every small area around the point.  

The first two thoughts are the well known way of saying without big changes with lines of numbers, which is the same as the usual small number thought and everyone says it is right.  

The third thought – that the thing turns sets of numbers that stay not too big into sets of numbers that stay not too big in small areas around the point – is true for all three case things and might look like a different needed thought. Yet it is not different. It comes from the line thought. If a thing keeps the idea that every line of numbers that gets closer to a point also gets closer after the thing, then it stays not too big in every small area around that point, and so it turns sets of numbers that stay not too big in that area into sets of numbers that stay not too big. The third thought adds no new thought that is already in the second. The case things were all good things that showed many nice things beyond just being without big changes, and the thought has taken one such nice thing – staying not too big – and raised it up as a different thought, when in truth it follows from the line thought as a clear end. The look at this has taken true words about without big changes things for a different thought, making the thought look more strong without adding any real new thought.
english_query: counterexample to: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point a if and only if (i) f is defined at a, (ii) for every sequence (x_n) converging to a, the sequence (f(x_n)) converges to f(a), and (iii) f maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of a.
ugf_response:
The first idea is that the statement says a rule that takes a number and gives another number does not have a jump at a certain point exactly when three things are true: the rule gives a result for that point, every list of numbers that gets closer and closer to that point makes the list of results get closer and closer to the result at the point, and the rule changes any group of numbers that stay inside some size into another group of numbers that also stay inside some size, no matter how small an area around the point we look at. The second idea is that the person shows a rule that follows the first two things but does not follow the third thing, yet the rule still has no jump at the point. In other words, they give a rule that for any list of numbers that gets closer to the point, the results also get closer, and the rule does give a result at the point, but there is a group of numbers that stays inside a size near the point whose results do not stay inside any size. This shows that the third condition is not needed for the rule to have no jump at the point, so the original statement is not true.

The third idea the person takes from this is that the original “if and only if” claim is false because they have found a rule that meets the first two requirements and still has no jump, while breaking the third requirement. They think that because this one rule works, the whole claim must be wrong.

The fourth idea is to judge whether this line of thinking is good. The example they give does indeed meet the first two requirements and still has no jump, so it correctly shows that the third requirement is not necessary. That means the claim that all three things are needed is false. The reasoning that a single example that breaks one part of the claim is enough to show the whole claim is false is sound, because “if and only if” means both ways must hold for every rule. Finding one rule where the third part fails while the other parts hold is enough to prove the whole statement does not work. So the thinking is good and the conclusion follows correctly from the example.

question: counterexample to: Perception guides action if and only if (i) S has a conscious perceptual experience of the relevant features of the environment, (ii) the content of this experience determines S's motor response, and (iii) the accuracy of the action depends on the accuracy of the perceptual experience.
expected_answer:
Think about these cases of seeing leading to moving. When a person reaches out to take a cup, the seeing of where the cup is makes the hand go to the right place. When a person steps over the raised part of the road, the seeing of how high that part is makes the leg go over it. When a person catches a ball, the seeing of where the ball is going makes the hands go to the right place. In each case, the person has a mind feeling of the parts of the world that matter, that feeling makes the move, and how right the move is comes from how right the feeling is. From these cases one could say: seeing makes moving only when the person has a mind feeling of the parts that matter, that feeling makes the move, and how right the move is comes from how right the feeling is.

This idea, however, is met by a strong brain break. Think about a person who cannot know the shape and way of things she looks at. She cannot say if a hole goes up and down or left and right; she cannot tell or show the things she sees. Yet when she is asked to put a piece of paper through the hole, she puts it in the right way easy. When she is asked to take a thing, her hand opens the right space for the thing big or small. Her moves are led by what she sees with very right, but she has no mind feeling of the parts that matter. The first part does not happen at all. The earlier cases used people who could see and also feel the things, and the saying made the mind feeling a must. The brain case shows that the join is maybe not always needed: seeing can lead to moving through ways that go around the mind feeling.
english_query: counterexample to: Perception guides action if and only if (i) S has a conscious perceptual experience of the relevant features of the environment, (ii) the content of this experience determines S's motor response, and (iii) the accuracy of the action depends on the accuracy of the perceptual experience.
ugf_response:
The first thought is about a statement that says a person’s seeing or feeling of things leads to their doing only when three things are true. The three things are: first, the person must be aware of the thing they see or feel that matters for what they are about to do; second, what they see or feel must make them pick a certain move; third, how well the move works must rely on how true the seeing or feeling was.

The second thought gives a different example that is meant to show the statement can be wrong. In this example a person moves a lever after a flash of light, but the person does not actually notice the flash; the move happens because the light triggers a hidden part of the body that works without the person’s awareness. The move still works well, even though the person was not aware of the flash and the flash did not decide the move. The third thought is the claim that because the facts in the example are true, the original three‑part statement must be false. The reasoning goes: if a move can happen correctly without the person being aware of the thing they see or feel, then the first part of the three‑part rule is not needed; if the move can happen without the thing they see or feel deciding it, then the second part is not needed; and if the move can be correct even when the thing they see or feel is not true, then the third part is not needed. Therefore the whole rule does not hold.

The fourth thought looks at whether that reasoning is good. The step from the facts to the claim that the whole rule is false is shaky because the example only shows that one of the three parts can be missing in one special case. It does not prove that the other two parts are never needed, nor that the rule cannot work in other situations. A better view might be that the rule works for many cases but that there are rare cases where a move can happen without all three parts. So the thinking that the example completely knocks out the rule is not fully convincing, even though the facts of the example are clear.

question: counterexample to: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one, and (iii) n minus one is not divisible by n.
expected_answer:
Suppose a person says a count number we are looking at is a first kind of number only if the number we are looking at is more than one, the number we are looking at cannot be made by putting two count numbers each more than one together, and the thing we get when we take away one from the number we are looking at cannot be cut into parts that are the same.  

The first two needs are clear: the number must be more than one, and it must not be broken into smaller parts. These are the usual needs.  

The third need, however, needs a close look.  

Think about any count number that is more than one. The thing we get when we take away one from that number is always less than that number, and a smaller count number is never able to be cut into parts that are the same as a bigger one.  

So the thing we get when we take away one from that number cannot be cut into parts that are the same as that number for every count number that is more than one, no matter if it is a first kind of number or made of smaller parts.  

The need is very easily true and does not leave out any things at all.  

It looks like it puts a stop, but it does not.  

Someone might see that a good explain that has a need that does nothing is broken not because it gives wrong answers — it does not — but because it shows the form of the idea wrong when it tries to look at it.  

A not careful reader may think that the third need does real work, when in fact the whole hard work is on the first two needs.  

A good explain of first kind of number should have only needs that each are needed and together enough, with no extra words that only make it look hard.
english_query: counterexample to: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one, and (iii) n minus one is not divisible by n.
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is a description of a whole number that they call “special.” They say a whole number is special if three things are true: first, the number is bigger than one; second, you cannot write the number as a result of putting together two whole numbers that are each bigger than one; third, if you take one away from the number, the result cannot be split into equal groups that each have the size of the original number. They present these three points as true statements about what makes a number special.

From those three points they think they can decide whether a number is special or not. They say that if a number follows all three points, then it must be special, and if they can find a number that follows the three points but is not special, that would show the description is wrong. To try this, they look at the number two. Two is bigger than one, you cannot put together two whole numbers each bigger than one to get two, and if you take one away you get one, which cannot be split into equal groups that each have size two. Because the three points are true for two, they say the rule says two should be special, but they claim two is not special. They then say this mismatch is a case that shows the rule does not work.

The thinking has a problem. The first two points are indeed the usual way to tell if a whole number is special, but the third point does not add any new information: for any whole number bigger than one, taking one away will always give a result that cannot be split into equal groups of the original size. So the third point is true for every such number and does not help to separate special numbers from non‑special ones. Moreover, the claim that two is not special is false; by the usual meaning, two does satisfy the first two points and is therefore special. Because the claim about two is wrong, the case they present does not actually break the description. In short, the first idea is partly true, the second idea tries to use a false claim to prove the description wrong, and the reasoning does not hold up.

question: counterexample to: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the land for the requisite statutory period, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been adverse to the interests of the true owner — that is, without the true owner's permission.
expected_answer:
Consider three case where a person gets a right to ground by long use. A person who grows food marks the edge of a piece of empty ground, turns over the ground, and uses it open for twenty years; at the end of that time people call him the one who has the ground. A person who lives in an empty house, makes it better, and lives there open and all the time for twenty years; people call his right true. A person who works on a boat uses a piece of the side of a river, ties his boat there and makes his catching things dry there, all open and without hide or fight for twenty years; people call his right true. In each case the holding is all the time, open, and against the true one who has the ground okay – that is, without the true one who has the ground okay. From these case one can make an idea: a person gets a right by long use if and only if his holding is all the time for the needed time, open and well known, and against the true one who has the ground wishes.

But one must think about a harder case. Suppose a person lives on a piece of ground next to his own ground because he really but wrong thought it is part of the ground he has. A line that shows the side was drawn wrong on the big paper, and the person, using the paper, uses the piece as his own. He marks the edge, grows things on it, and keeps it for the needed time. His holding is all the time and not stopped. It is open and well known; all the close people can see that he uses the piece. And it is against the true one who has the ground because he does not have the true one who has the ground okay; the true one who has the ground lives far away and does not know about the taking. The three parts of the idea seem to be met. Yet the kind of his holding is different from the person who grows food and knew he was taking another person empty ground. The person who grows food used the ground as his own even though he knew it was not; his holding was a say against the true one who has the ground. The person who uses the wrong paper also uses the ground as his own, but without any thought that it is against the true one who has the ground; he does not know he is taking. The earlier case were all times when the person knew he was saying another person empty ground – times when the say was both without okay and the person used the ground as his own. The idea said the no okay but did not say the person also must use the ground as his own – a part that is different from just the no okay and that the idea says is needed for the time of the long use idea.
english_query: counterexample to: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the land for the requisite statutory period, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been adverse to the interests of the true owner — that is, without the true owner's permission.
ugf_response: The first idea that the person making the case says is true is that there is a situation where one person has been using a piece of land for a long stretch of time, has never stopped using it, has used it in a way that anyone can see, and has used it without asking the person who really has the right to own that land. The case says that even with all of those things the person who has been using the land does not get the right to own it. The second idea that follows from that fact is that the rule that says “if a person has used land for a long time, openly, and without the real owner’s permission, then that person gets the right to own the land” must be wrong, because the example shows a case where the three things happen but the right to own does not appear. The third idea is an evaluation of whether that thinking is good. To decide if the thinking is good we have to check whether the example truly meets all three parts of the rule. If the example really does have the long use, the open use, and the use without permission, then the example does show that the rule is not enough by itself and the thinking that the rule is false is strong. But if the example is missing even one of those parts—for instance if the use was not truly open to everyone, or if the real owner had given some hidden permission, or if the use was not truly without stopping—then the example does not break the rule and the thinking that the rule is false would be weak. So the quality of the reasoning depends on whether the example really fits all three conditions; if it does, the reasoning is good, and if it does not, the reasoning is not convincing.

question: counterexample to: An entity W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the boundaries of W, (ii) the parts P1, ..., Pn jointly exhaust W — that is, there is no region of W that is not occupied by some Pi, and (iii) the parts do not overlap — that is, no spatial region is occupied by more than one Pi.
expected_answer:
One might say that a thing is a whole made of parts only when each part stays inside the whole, the parts together fill the whole with no empty places left, and the parts do not cover the same space.  

This idea makes a clear look: a whole is like a look made of pieces that fit together with no empty places and no parts that share the same space.  

The idea that each part stays inside makes sure no part goes outside the whole.  

The idea that the parts together fill the whole makes sure the whole is completely covered.  

And the idea that parts do not share the same space makes sure each space of the whole belongs to only one part.  

But some may see that this look of parts is too hard for many wholes we see in the real world.  

Think about a human body and its work parts. The blood group and the feeling group are both parts of the body.  

Each stays inside the body.  

Together with the bone group, the moving group, the food group, and other parts, they together fill the body.  

But they share some of the same space: blood lines go through feeling part, small blood lines go into the head and the back bone, and you cannot put one of these parts in a place without the other also being there.  

The idea that parts do not share space does not work here, but no one would say that the blood group and the feeling group are not parts of the body.  

The idea comes from the look of cutting a thing into parts that do not share space, but the real way a living thing is made is into work parts that can go through each other free.  

What we need is a big idea of being a part that lets parts share space when we tell them different by what they do, not by where they are.
english_query: counterexample to: An entity W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the boundaries of W, (ii) the parts P1, ..., Pn jointly exhaust W — that is, there is no region of W that is not occupied by some Pi, and (iii) the parts do not overlap — that is, no spatial region is occupied by more than one Pi.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward says that a whole thing is made of a group of pieces when three things are true. First, each piece must be inside the edges of the whole. Second, the pieces together must fill every part of the whole so that there is no place inside the whole that is left empty. Third, the pieces must not share the same place inside the whole; no spot can belong to more than one piece at the same time. The claim says that these three points are both needed and enough for something to be called a whole made of parts.

The second idea gives an example that is meant to show that the three points are not enough. In this example there is a thing that looks like a whole and is made of several pieces that are each inside its edges, that together fill all the space inside, and that do not share any spot. Yet, when we look at the thing we see that it does not behave like a normal whole made of parts – for instance it may act as a single piece itself or it may have a property that the three points do not explain. Because this example seems to follow the three points but still does not fit the idea of a whole made of parts, the example is used to argue that the three points cannot be the whole story.

Looking at the thinking behind the example, the step from the first idea to the second is a move that tries to show the rule is wrong by finding a case that meets the rule but seems to break the meaning of a whole. This move is a common way to test a rule, and it works well when the example really does meet all three points. If the example does meet the three points, then the reasoning that the rule is not enough is strong. However, if the example actually fails one of the three points in a way that is not obvious – for example if two pieces do share a tiny spot or if a tiny part of the whole is not covered – then the move is not strong because the example would not truly follow the rule. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the example truly satisfies the three conditions. If it does, the thinking is good and shows the rule needs more detail. If it does not, the thinking is weak because it attacks a rule with a case that does not really fit the rule.

question: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S is composed of atoms all having the same atomic number, (ii) S has a characteristic set of physical and chemical properties — a definite melting point, density, reactivity, and so forth — that distinguish it from all other elements, and (iii) S exists as a single, uniform phase under standard conditions — that is, S is homogeneous.
expected_answer:
Think about three things that we call a kind of single part thing.  

All the tiny parts of the first thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is soft, yellow, metal, does not get wear away easily, it is made only of tiny parts that all have the number 79, and it has a set point where it turns to a hot soft form, a way it is heavy for its size, and a way it likes to change with other things that make it different from any other thing.  

All the tiny parts of the second thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is hard, dark, metal, it is made of tiny parts that all have the number 26, and it also has its own set of things it does.  

All the tiny parts of the third thing are the same kind and there are no other things together. It is an air like thing with no color and no smell that is needed for breathing, it is made of tiny parts that all have the number 8.  

Each thing is made of tiny parts of one kind, has its own set of things it does, and is all the same way all over when we see it in the usual way.  

We could write a thought: a thing is a single part thing if it is made of tiny parts that all have the same number, it has its own set of things it does, and it is all the same way all over when we see it in the usual way.  

Now think about the thing with the number 6. Any person who study things would call it a single part thing. Yet the thing does not have just one set of things it does.  

The hard clear form of the thing with number 6 – tiny parts put together so each tiny part is put together with four others in a shape that goes in three ways – is the hardest thing we know, clear, very bright, and it does not let force move, and it needs a very high hot point where it turns to a hot soft form.  

The soft dark form of the thing with number 6 – tiny parts put together in flat sheets that look like many little flat pieces – is one of the softest hard things we know, not clear, dark, and so good at letting force move that we use it in pieces that help move force.  

The two forms have different ways they are heavy for their size, different ways the tiny parts are put together, different ways they look with light, and different ways they let force move.  

When we look at them with our eyes, they are as different as two things can be.  

In the usual way we see it, the thing with number 6 is not all the same way all over; it can be the hard clear form or it can be the soft dark form, and both are okay to call it the thing with number 6.  

The second and third parts of the thought are not true.  

The earlier cases were things that happen to be only one usual form – one form of the thing with number 79, one form of the thing with number 26, one form of the thing with number 8 – and the thought took this same as needed.  

The thing with number 6 shows that what makes a thing a single part thing is not the way it looks or acts on a big size but the fact that its tiny parts are the same, and that one single part thing can look very different on how its tiny parts are put together.
english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S is composed of atoms all having the same atomic number, (ii) S has a characteristic set of physical and chemical properties — a definite melting point, density, reactivity, and so forth — that distinguish it from all other elements, and (iii) S exists as a single, uniform phase under standard conditions — that is, S is homogeneous.

The third idea is an assessment of the thinking. The first idea is a clear statement of what they think a basic part is.
