question: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs naturally on Earth.
expected_answer:
One can say a thing that is a kind of stuff that cannot be broken by normal ways, whose tiny parts all have the same number of tiny push parts, and that is found on the ground we live on.  

This way of talking about it holds the old group of things like the light stuff we breathe, the light stuff we breathe, the bright metal, the strong metal, with true.  

But a story that goes against the third thing we check can be taken from the past of big small part study.  

Think about the thing with the number 43.  

It cannot be broken into simpler stuff by normal ways.  

All of its tiny parts have 43 push parts.  

But it is not found on the ground we live on in any big enough size.  

The thing with number 43 was first made in 1937 by hitting the thing with number 42 with fast tiny parts in a big machine that makes fast tiny parts.  

Its life on the ground we live on is all made by people.  

The third thing we check is broken.  

But the thing with number 43 is for sure a thing that is a kind of stuff.  

It sits in its spot in the big group of things with numbers between the thing with number 42 and the thing with number 44, makes together parts that can be guessed, and acts in every way the thing with number 43 should act.  

It seems that the way we say it, by saying it must be found on the ground we live on, adds a place stop into what is really an idea of simple stuff.  

A thing is said by its number, not by where or how it comes to be found.
english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs naturally on Earth.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person puts forward is that there is a thing we call a basic part of matter that cannot be broken down by any chemical way, that is made of only one kind of tiny center bit, and that we have not found this thing in nature on our planet. They point to a specific basic part that scientists have made in a lab but that does not appear naturally. The claim that this lab‑made basic part cannot be broken down by chemistry is true, because no chemical process can split a basic part into something simpler. The claim that all of its tiny atoms have the same number of center bits is also true, because that is how we define a basic part. The claim that it does not occur naturally on our planet is true for the newest basic parts that have only been produced by people. So the facts they give about this lab‑made basic part are correct.

The second idea they draw from those facts is that the statement “a thing is a basic part of matter if and only if it meets the three conditions” must be wrong. Because they have found a thing that meets the first two conditions but not the third, yet we still call it a basic part, they say this shows the “only if” side of the statement cannot be right. If a single example can be found that breaks a claim that says “all things that have these three features are the only things that are basic parts,” then the whole claim is false. In that sense the reasoning that one counter‑example is enough to knock down an “if and only if” claim is a good piece of thinking.

The third idea they suggest, without spelling it out, is that we should change the definition to leave out the need for a thing to be found in nature. Whether that step is good depends on whether we want a definition that only talks about the inner make‑up of the thing and not about where we can find it. The reasoning that the example forces a change in the definition is reasonable, because the example does show the old definition does not match what scientists now call a basic part. However, the decision to drop the natural‑occurrence condition also has to think about why that condition was added in the first place – perhaps to keep the word from being used for things that are only made in a lab. So the thinking that the example shows a problem with the old rule is solid, while the judgment about how to fix the rule needs a little more discussion.

question: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) the material standard of living of the majority of S's members has risen during P, (ii) the range of civil liberties available to S's members has expanded during P, and (iii) S's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P.
expected_answer:
One may say that real past change is made of more good things for people, more ways to act for people, and more ways to make and share ideas. This way of talking shows the money, the way people are told what to do, and the thinking parts that we most often join with the idea of change.  

But a story that shows this not enough can be taken from the past of big machine life.  

Imagine a group of people, over a long time of many years, see a big rise in the good life for most of them: food is more, homes are safer, and sick is less. At the same time, the number of ways to act grows: the chance to choose who leads is given to more, things that stop what people say are less, and laws that keep people safe are stronger. And the ways of the group to make and share ideas get very big because of new schools, the making of pages that are made by pressing letters, and the start of rooms with books that anyone can use. If we do not have that ask, a group may meet every part of change while slowly breaking the place of its own staying good.
english_query: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) the material standard of living of the majority of S's members has risen during P, (ii) the range of civil liberties available to S's members has expanded during P, and (iii) S's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P.

The second idea the person says follows from those facts is that the statement “a group has real forward change over a time only when those three things all happen” must be wrong. The third idea is an assessment of whether the thinking is good. The reasoning is a kind of test where one example that does not fit the rule is used to say the rule is false. question: counterexample to: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, (ii) the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and (iii) the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.

Think about three ideas that try to show that a god is real. The first idea looks at the whole world and says there must be a first cause. The second idea looks at the way living things seem to be made with a plan and says there must be a person who makes. The third idea looks at the fact that right and wrong seem the same for everyone and says there must be a higher right place. Each idea starts with looking at the world – its being there, its order, its right and wrong – and ends with the thought that a god is real. From these ideas one could make a strong god idea: it starts with clear looking points, the guess of a god cause is the only idea that makes sense, and the end point says what the god is like.

But this way of calling a god idea, taken from ideas that start with looking at the world, is too small. The other ideas were all from looking at the world; the way we called a god idea used their looking part as a must, when this only thought idea shows that a only thought road to a god can be and still kept.
english_query: counterexample to: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, (ii) the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and (iii) the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.

Second, the step that says the big being is the only good reason for those facts must be the only good reason we can think of. Third, the end of the talk must say what the big being is like.

The second idea in the case is that there is a different case that shows the three things are not needed for a talk to be strong. The thinking in the second idea is pretty good if the different case really does show a strong talk without all three parts. question: counterexample to: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the events constituting T have taken place, (ii) T is no longer present — no part of T is occurring now, and (iii) T is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it.
expected_answer: Someone could say that a time is in the past only when it has happened, it is not now, and it is kept in the memory of at least one mind that felt it. That idea has a kind of easy feeling. When we think about things that happened before — a day when we were small and had food, a walk in the morning on a day before, a paper that people signed — we think about things that have happened, that are not happening now, and that are kept in the memory of the people who saw them. Memory looks like the way the past stays alive; without it, someone could think the past would be nothing — just an empty space where something used to be. But we must think about how the earth was made. That thing happened many many years ago, far before any mind was there. Hot rock came together from a cloud of dust and air; the outside got cold; the first hard outside part became hard over many years. These things have happened and are not now. They are, by any good story, in the past. But no mind saw them; no memory keeps them. We guess they happened from the rock story — from what rocks are made of, from the way rock parts change over time, from space rocks — but this guess is not memory. Memory needs a mind that was there at the thing and that keeps a thought of it, and there was no mind like that when the earth was made. The third part of that idea does not work. The idea has joined together the way we most often understand the past — memory — with what makes something past. A time does not need a person who saw it to have happened and to be not now; the past goes far beyond what any mind can touch, and most of what has happened happened without any mind to see it.
english_query: counterexample to: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the events constituting T have taken place, (ii) T is no longer present — no part of T is occurring now, and (iii) T is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person is saying is that there is a case where a time point has happened, is not happening now, and is kept in the mind of at least one aware person, yet we would not call that time point part of the past. They point to a situation like a story that is being told over and over again, where the event is still alive in the telling, so they claim the event is not really past even though it meets the three facts. The second idea they draw from this is that because this case seems to fit the three facts but does not feel like a past time point, the rule that says a time point is past if and only if those three facts are true must be wrong. In other words, they think the rule does not match what we actually think about time.

To see if the thinking is good, we first check whether the example really does have all three facts. The event did happen, it is not happening now, and at least one aware person remembers it, so the three facts are true. Next we ask whether the feeling that the event is still alive in a story means it is not past. The feeling that something is still being talked about does not change the fact that the event itself is finished and not happening now. It only means that the memory of the event is being used in a new way. So the jump from “the event is still talked about” to “the event is not past” does not follow. The reasoning mixes the state of the event with the way we talk about it, and that mix makes the conclusion weak. Therefore, while the first idea is correct about the three facts, the second idea that the rule is false does not hold up because the example does not actually break the rule; it only shows a different way we think about the event after it has happened. The thinking is therefore not a good proof that the rule is wrong.

question: counterexample to: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species, (ii) B appears without prior learning or training, and (iii) B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern that does not vary across individuals or occasions.
expected_answer: One might say that a way a thing acts is without learning if it is shown by all normal members of the group, shows up without learning, and is done in the same way each time. This idea catches the ways most often called without learning: the making of a thin line by a small many leg thing, the rolling of eggs by gray birds, the drinking of white drink by baby warm blood animal. In each case the way is for all, not learned, and done in the usual, same each time way. Yet a case that shows this idea is not enough is given by song of song birds. The first kind of song is without learning: a young bird kept away from sound will make a simple but still known kind of the group usual song. The song shows up without clear teaching – no bird teaches another to sing the way a person teaches a child to read. It is shown by all normal grown birds. The first thing and the second thing are true. But the song is not done in the same way each time. Young birds must hear grown bird song during a time when they can learn a lot to grow the full kind of song, and each bird makes its own different ways – different sounds in different places, and extra parts that let people who have heard many songs know which bird it is. The third thing is not true. Yet people who study animals still call bird song without learning. One might see that the idea was built from the case of the simple, same each time move – the quick bend of a knee, the quick change of eye. Many of the most important ways without learning in the world have a first kind that is made more clear by what they have lived through, allowing a lot of different ways for each one while staying clearly the usual way for that group.
english_query: counterexample to: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species, (ii) B appears without prior learning or training, and (iii) B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern that does not vary across individuals or occasions.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is put forward says that a kind of action can be called natural only when three things are true. First, the action is done by the whole group of the kind. Second, the action shows up without any teaching before it happens. Third, the action is done in the same way each time for each one and does not change from one time to another. The person giving the case says they have an example that fits all three of those points but that most people would not call that action natural. They point to a type of animal that always builds a place to live in the same way, does it without ever being shown how, and does it the same way for every animal and every time. Because this example seems to meet the three points, they say the definition that ties natural action to those three points must be wrong or at least incomplete.

The second idea they think follows is that if one example can meet the three points and still not be thought of as natural, then the rule that says “only when the three points are true the action is natural” cannot be correct. From this they conclude that the rule needs to be changed, either by adding more conditions or by removing some of the existing ones, so that the rule matches what we actually think of as natural actions.

The thinking behind this argument can be looked at in a few ways. It is good that they try to test a rule by looking for a case that might break it, because that is a common way to see if a rule works. However, the strength of the argument depends on whether the example truly meets all three points. If the whole group does not always do the action, or if some teaching or copying happens early in life, then the example does not really match the three points. If the way the action is done can change a little from one animal to another, then the third point is not fully met either. Because those details can be hard to know for sure, the claim that the example definitely breaks the rule may be on shaky ground. So the overall reasoning is useful as a way to question the rule, but it may not be enough by itself to prove that the rule is false unless the example can be shown to satisfy the three points without any doubt.

question: counterexample to: A person S possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) S regularly acts in the face of danger, (ii) S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse, and (iii) S's actions in the face of danger are voluntary.

question: counterexample to: A person S is entitled to restitution from a person T if and only if (i) T has been unjustly enriched at S's expense, (ii) there is no legal justification for T's retention of the enrichment, and (iii) S has suffered a corresponding loss.

question: counterexample to: A set A is larger than a set B if and only if (i) A contains more elements than B in the sense that A has elements not in B, (ii) B is a proper subset of A, and (iii) A is not in one-to-one correspondence with B.
expected_answer:
A big change in the idea about never ending groups showed that the feeling idea of “bigger” needs to be changed when it is used on never ending groups. The counting numbers hold all the counting numbers and more, yet a way to put each thing in one group with exactly one thing in the other group puts them in the same size. Only the never ending numbers get away from this way of putting each thing with exactly one thing. The special way of looking at a group of numbers written in order shows that any group of the never ending numbers can not hold them all.

Someone might try to say that one group is bigger than another group if the first group has some things that are not in the second, the second group is a group that is inside the first and not the same, and the first group can not be put each thing with exactly one thing in the second.
