But a case that shows the idea is not enough shows up right away with the counting numbers and the numbers that can be made by taking a counting number and making it two times bigger. The numbers that can be made by taking a counting number and making it two times bigger are a group that is inside the counting numbers, and the counting numbers have things – the numbers that are not even – that are not in the group of even numbers. Yet the counting numbers and the even numbers can be put each thing with exactly one thing by the way that changes each counting number into the number that is two times bigger. The first two ideas work but the third does not, so the idea in a right way does not say the counting numbers are bigger.

But the idea would not work for a case where one group is bigger but is not a group that holds all the things of the other – a case with the never ending numbers and the counting numbers, where the inside group idea does not matter. What the other cases show is that the size of a group is decided only by whether a way of putting each thing with exactly one thing can be made. The inside group idea is a thing that seems important but is not needed or enough for not the same size. The idea, by using the inside group idea as a way, puts together two ideas that do not need each other.
english_query: counterexample to: A set A is larger than a set B if and only if (i) A contains more elements than B in the sense that A has elements not in B, (ii) B is a proper subset of A, and (iii) A is not in one-to-one correspondence with B.
ugf_response:
First, the idea that is being looked at says that one group A is bigger than another group B only when three things are true. The first thing is that A has some things that B does not have. The second thing is that B sits inside A but is not the same as A. The third thing is that you cannot find a way to pair each thing in A with one different thing in B so that every thing in both groups gets a partner. 

Second, the case that is given to show the idea does not always work uses two groups that go on forever. One group is all whole numbers. The other group is all the whole numbers that are even. The even group is inside the whole group and it is not the same as the whole group, so the first two things are true. It also has things that the even group does not have, such as the number one. But you can still pair each whole number with an even number by matching each number n with the even number 2 × n. That means there is a way to give every thing in the first group a partner in the second group, so the third thing is not true. Because the third thing fails, the original idea would say that A is not bigger, but the case still shows that the three things can happen together even when the groups are the same size in the sense that you can pair them up. 

Third, the thinker says that because the three things happen in this case, the original claim that those three things are enough to tell when one group is bigger must be wrong. They think the case proves that the claim does not work for all groups. 

Finally, the thinking is partly right. The three things do tell when one group is bigger if the groups stop after a certain point, because then you cannot pair them up if one really has more things. But when the groups go on forever, you can have a group that sits inside another and still be able to pair them up, so the three things are not enough by themselves. The reasoning therefore shows a limit to the original claim: it works for groups that end, but it does not work for groups that keep going. The case is a good example of that limit, so the thinking that the claim needs more conditions is a good one.

question: counterexample to: A person P perceives an object O if and only if (i) P has a sensory experience with phenomenal content that represents O, (ii) P can attend to O and discriminate O from its surroundings, and (iii) P can identify O — that is, P can say what O is.
expected_answer: One might say that a person sees a thing only when the person has a feeling that shows the thing, the person can look at the thing and tell it different from the things around, and the person can name the thing. The words try to cover the case of a clear, known seeing. When I see a red bird sitting on a branch, I have a sight that shows the bird, I can look at it and tell it different from the green leaves, and I can name it – I can say that it is a red bird. Yet we must think about the case of a person walking through a thick and new group of trees. From somewhere on the ground comes a sound – a low, on and off noise that goes up and down with no order. The person stops and listens. He has a feeling that shows the sound; he hears it clear. He can look at it and tell it different from the other sounds of the world – from the wind in the top above, from the far call of a bird, from the sound of a branch moving. But he cannot name it. He does not know what makes the sound. Is it an animal moving through the leaves? Is it the wind shaking the ground? Is it water moving over stones under the leaf floor? He cannot say. He sees the sound – he hears it as clear as he hears his own steps – but he does not know what it is. The last part of the words does not work. The look has asked that seeing also must be able to name, which is more than what seeing alone gives. Seeing gives us feeling of things and things that happen in our world; naming – the putting of what is seen into a name – is a more thinking work that needs what you know, feeling, and ideas that the person might not have. One can see what one cannot name.
english_query: counterexample to: A person P perceives an object O if and only if (i) P has a sensory experience with phenomenal content that represents O, (ii) P can attend to O and discriminate O from its surroundings, and (iii) P can identify O — that is, P can say what O is.
ugf_response:
The first idea is a rule that says a person sees a thing only when three things happen at the same time. The first of those three things is that the person has a feeling inside that stands for the thing. The second thing is that the person can look at the thing and tell it apart from what is around it. The third thing is that the person can say what the thing is.

The second idea is an example that tries to show the rule does not work. In the example a person looks at a shape on a wall in a dark room. The person can look at the shape and tell it apart from the dark, but the person does not have a clear feeling inside that stands for the shape, and the person cannot say exactly what the shape is. Still the person says they see the shape.

The third idea is the claim that because this example shows a person can see a thing even when one of the three things is missing, the rule must be wrong. This claim depends on whether the example really does show a case where the person sees the thing. If the person’s feeling inside was only vague, some might say it still counts as a feeling that stands for the thing, so the first requirement is still met. If the person’s words about the thing are very weak, some might say the third requirement is only partly met. The reasoning is only as good as the way the example is described. If the example truly lacks one of the three things, then the reasoning that the rule is wrong is strong. If the example still has all three things in a hidden way, then the reasoning is weak. So the quality of the thinking rests on how well the example actually breaks any of the three parts of the rule.

question: counterexample to: A person P trusts a person Q if and only if (i) P and Q are personally acquainted — that is, P has direct knowledge of Q's character gained through repeated personal interaction, (ii) P has observed Q acting reliably in the past and infers that Q will act reliably in the future, and (iii) the trust is reciprocal — Q also trusts P.
expected_answer:
Think about three things where trust is simple. A man gives money to his neighbor, trusting the money will be paid back. His trust comes from knowing each other; he has known the neighbor for many years, has seen him when they work together, and has found him always on time with what he must do. A person who sells things lets a long time buyer take things now, trusting the buyer is good, which he has had many chances to see over many years of buying and selling. A person who grows food puts his work things in an open small building, trusting that his neighbors — men he has known since he was a child — will take care of his things. From these things you could make a simple saying: a person trusts another if and only if they know each other by meeting many times, the first has seen the second act in a good way before, and the trust goes both ways.

But we also need to think about the trust that a person who rides in a big flying thing puts in the person who flies the big flying thing. The person who rides comes to the place where big flying things start, shows his paper that lets him go, walks through the door where people get on, sits down, puts the thing that holds his pants on, and gives his life — with no worry and no fear — to the person who will fly the big flying thing. Yet the person who rides has never met the person who flies. He does not know the name of the person who flies, has no knowing of the ways of the person who flies, and has never seen the person who flies do any act, good or bad. His trust does not come from knowing a person or seeing before; it comes from something else — from the things that tell how to act for people who fly big things, from the fact that the person who flies has been taught, looked at, and given the yes by a group that makes the things that tell how to act, from the fact that the big flying thing has been looked at and said good, and from the fact that the group that runs the trips must follow the things that tell how to act for being safe made by law. The trust does not go both ways in any real way; the person who flies does not give the safe of the person who rides, and the way they are joined is not the same for both. All three parts of the simple saying do not hold. Yet the trust is real and very strong — as strong as any trust between neighbors, because it is a trust where the life of the person is in a chance of a bad thing.

The earlier things were taken from places where people meet each other, where trust is about a person, seen by watching, and goes both ways, and the simple saying took these parts as important. The big flying thing shows that the trust that holds up new life together is mostly not about a person, is made by groups, and is one way, and it does not rest on knowing the ways of a person but on the good work of many parts, looks, and the things that tell how to act that the person who trusts may never have looked at and could not decide.
english_query: counterexample to: A person P trusts a person Q if and only if (i) P and Q are personally acquainted — that is, P has direct knowledge of Q's character gained through repeated personal interaction, (ii) P has observed Q acting reliably in the past and infers that Q will act reliably in the future, and (iii) the trust is reciprocal — Q also trusts P.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being put forward is a claim about when one person will trust another. It says that a person will trust another exactly when three things are true. The first thing is that the two people must know each other in a face to face way, that is, one has met the other many times and has a clear picture of how the other acts. The second thing is that the first person must have seen the other act in a good and steady way before and must think that the other will keep acting in that good way in the future. The third thing is that the trust must go both ways, so the other person also trusts the first.

The second idea that is being offered is a particular situation that is said to break the claim. In this situation, a person A trusts a person B even though they have never met face to face and have never seen B act in a steady way before. A may have heard about B from a third person or may have read a note that says B is trustworthy, and A decides to trust B. At the same time, B does not trust A back. The person who brings up the situation says that because this situation exists, the claim that all three things are needed for trust cannot be true.

The third idea is the judgment about whether the thinking is good. If the situation really does show a case where trust exists without the first two things and without the trust being both ways, then it does show that the claim does not hold in all cases. That would make the thinking good, because a single case that breaks any part of the claim is enough to show the claim is not always correct. However, if the situation actually still fits the three things—for example, if A’s knowledge of B through the third person is still a kind of face to face knowledge, or if B’s lack of trust does not matter because the claim only talked about the trust that A feels—then the situation does not truly break the claim and the thinking would be weak. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the situation truly leaves out the three required parts of the claim. If it does, the thinking is strong; if it does not, the thinking is not strong.

question: counterexample to: A set of sentences is consistent if and only if there exists a model that satisfies all members of the set, no sentence in the set is the negation of another, and the set is finite.
expected_answer: One way to look at the thought of a group of lines that all work together is this: a group is called the thought that all its lines can be true at the same time if there is a picture that makes each line work, no line says the not of another line, and the group has an end. The first point is the most well known and the most liked by many people. The second point can be seen as enough (though maybe not needed) promise against a clear kind of the thought that something says the not of something else. The third point – having an end – might be thought of because groups with no end bring hard things that the thought of the first point is not made to fit. Yet this point is clearly too small. Let us look at the group of all proved things of the first count number system. This group has no end, and so the thought we first gave would say it is not the thought that all its lines can be true at the same time. But it is the thought: the count numbers, in their normal way, make a picture that makes every proved thing of the system work. No thing that says the not of a thing that also says the not of a thing can be found. The group is, by every makes sense point, the thought that all its lines can be true at the same time. So we must see that the having an end point is not a real point of the thought that all its lines can be true at the same time and that the first thought, by adding it, keeps out things that clearly should be put in. The not enough of the way we say what something is is shown.
english_query: counterexample to: A set of sentences is consistent if and only if there exists a model that satisfies all members of the set, no sentence in the set is the negation of another, and the set is finite.
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is a claim about a rule for a group of sentences. They say that a group of sentences can all be true together if there is some situation that makes every one of them true, if no sentence in the group says the opposite of another sentence, and if the group is not endless. They then say that this claim is not always right and they give an example that shows it can fail.

The second idea is the example they use to try to show the claim is wrong. In the example they pick a small group of sentences that meets the two conditions they listed: there is no sentence that is the opposite of another, and the group is not endless. Yet they say that there is no situation that can make all of those sentences true at the same time. From this they think it follows that the original claim cannot be true in general.

The third part is a look at whether the thinking is good. If the example really does have no opposite sentences and really is a small group, and if indeed there is no situation that can make all of them true, then the example does show that the rule as stated does not hold for every possible group of sentences. In that case the step from the example to the claim that the rule is false is a solid step. If, however, the example slips on one of the needed points – for instance if one of the sentences can be seen as the opposite of another, or if the group is actually endless, or if there actually is a situation that makes them all true – then the step is weak and the claim that the rule is false would not be supported. So the quality of the thinking depends on how well the example fits the two stated conditions, and if it does, the reasoning is good.

question: counterexample to: An omission O by a person P is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) P had a duty to act, (ii) H would not have occurred had P fulfilled that duty, and (iii) P's failure to act was the sole cause of H.
expected_answer: One might say that a person can be said to have a must do thing when the person had a must do thing, the bad thing would not have happened if the must do thing had been done, and the not doing was the only thing that made the bad thing happen. The first two parts show the main idea of a must do thing for not doing: there is a must do thing and the bad thing would change if the must do thing was not done. The third part, saying it must be the only thing that made the bad thing happen, might look like it makes us point to the right person. But this part is too hard. Think of a person who works to watch the water at a water place and does not save a person who is going down in the water. The person who watches the water had a must do thing, and the person in the water would have lived if the person who watches the water had stepped in. But the bad thing had many helping things: the place had a not seen far down water part, another worker did not put up a sign that says bad, and a person who was there and could have thrown a thing that keeps a person up did not act. The not doing of the person who watches the water was not the only thing – it was one of many things happening at the same time. The third part does not work. Still no group of people who decide would clear the person who watches the water just because other things also helped the person go down. The law often says a person can be said to have a must do thing for not doing even when that not doing is needed but not enough by itself, because other things also help. The idea that asks for only one cause would end up keeping safe any person whose not doing happened together with other helping things – an end that would take away most of the law power for not doing.
english_query: counterexample to: An omission O by a person P is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) P had a duty to act, (ii) H would not have occurred had P fulfilled that duty, and (iii) P's failure to act was the sole cause of H.
ugf_response:
The first idea that is being said is that there is a rule about when a person’s not doing something can be called the reason for a hurt in the eyes of the law. The rule says that this can happen only if three points are true. The first point is that the person should have done something. The second point is that the hurt would not have happened if the person had done that thing. The third point is that the not doing is the only reason the hurt happened. The case that is being shown says that these three points can all be true and yet the rule still does not work. In the case, a person did not do something that he should have done, the hurt would not have happened if he had done it, and nothing else caused the hurt, but still the law does not call the not doing the reason for the hurt because another step in the chain of events was missing.

The second idea that the person who makes the case thinks follows from those facts is that the rule is not right. Because the case shows a situation where all three points are true but the rule would still say the not doing is not the reason, the maker of the case says that the rule must be changed or must be wrong. The maker then says that we need a better way to decide when a not doing is the reason for a hurt, one that can handle the kind of situation shown in the case.

The third idea is an evaluation of that thinking. The thinking is good in the sense that it points out a problem with the rule by using a clear example. It shows that if a rule says a not doing is the reason only when three things are true, there can be a real world picture where those three things are true and the rule still does not give the right answer. On the other hand, the thinking may miss that the law sometimes wants a link that is more than just the three things, such as a link that shows the not doing started a chain that could have been stopped. If that extra link is part of the law’s idea, then the case does not really break the rule but only shows a part of the rule that is not used in every situation. So the thinking is useful for showing a gap, but whether the gap means the rule must be thrown away or only fixed depends on how the law wants to think about the whole chain of events.

question: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.
expected_answer:
Think about three places that are like cases of a space. The inside of a room is a space: it has walls, a floor, and a top; it can hold things – a table, a chair, a light; and you can say where any thing is inside it by looking at the walls and the corners. A field is a space: it has side lines and end lines; people who play and a ball move inside it; and you can say where they are by using numbers that you count from the side lines. The inside of a box is a space: it has six sides; you can put things inside it; and you can give a place for a thing by using three directions. From these cases you can make a simple thought: a place is a space only when it has walls or sides, it can hold things, and you can tell where things are by using numbers.

But you also have to think about the open air above a flat sand. A person who flies a big flying thing starts from a small flat ground and goes to the west over a big flat sand that goes to the side all around. Above him the air goes up – light blue, then dark blue, then black high air. He is in a space; people would say it is. He moves in the space, changes where he is in it, and where he is can be said – by numbers that tell where – with as much close as the place of a chair in a room. Things are in this space: birds fly in it, clouds move in it, the big flying thing makes a line in it. Yet this space has no walls. There are no walls, no sides, no lines that mark an end. The sand goes to the side all around, and the air goes up with no top or wall to mark its end. You can not point to a wall and say “here the space ends.” The first part does not work. The first cases were all made by people with walls – rooms, fields, boxes – with clear walls, and the thought said walls are needed for a space. The open air shows that a place can be a space in the most full way – it can hold things, let things move, and you can tell where things are by numbers – even if it has no walls at all. The thought got the parts wrong.
english_query: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.
ugf_response:
The first idea the person says is that a place can be called a space only when three things are true. The first of those three things is that the place has limits or borders that separate it from what is outside. The second thing is that the place can hold things inside it. The third thing is that the location of each thing inside the place can be named by a small group of number places. The person also says that if those three things are true, then the place must be a space, and that if a place is a space, those three things must be true.

The second idea the person gives is a case that shows the first idea does not work. They point to a place that does have limits, can hold things, and where each thing’s location can be named by a small group of numbers, but which, for some reason, is not a space in the way the first idea describes. Or they point to a place that is a space but does not have one of the three things, such as a place that can hold things and has limits but where the locations cannot be named by a small group of numbers. In either form, the case is meant to show that the three things are not enough to decide when a place is a space, so the “if and only if” claim is broken.

The thinking that this case shows a problem with the first idea is a good piece of thinking. To show that a statement that says “A is true exactly when B, C and D are true” is false, it is enough to find one example where B, C and D are all true but A is not, or where A is true but one of B, C or D is not. The person does exactly that, so their reasoning is sound. Because they have a clear example that does not fit the claim, the claim that the three things are both necessary and sufficient for a place to be a space is not correct. Their argument therefore works, and the conclusion that the original claim is false follows correctly from the example they gave.

question: counterexample to: An act of naming succeeds in assigning a name N to an object O if and only if (i) a speaker utters N with the intention of designating O, (ii) the speaker is in perceptual contact with O at the time of the utterance, and (iii) the speaker has the concept of naming — that is, the speaker understands that the utterance is meant to establish a lasting designation.
expected_answer:
The acts of naming that we most easy see as good share a shape that simple talks often miss.  

When parents give a child a name at a baby naming day, the good of the naming needs not only the saying of a word that tries to point to the child, but also the people group and ideas that run the thing where the saying happens — a special day where people gather, a paper that writes down when a baby is born, people seeing the name.  

When a person who studies green things gives a new kind of green thing a two word name, the name works because it follows the set of ideas that tell what to do, the world wide set of name ideas, which gives power and makes it stay on the name.  

When a country gives a big bridge a name at a special day where people gather to give a name, the good needs the power given to the person who does the naming and the people group and ideas that run the work of many people.  

One may try to say this by calling good naming a saying made with the trying to point, with the thing being seen, by a person who says and knows about giving names.  

Yet a case that shows this idea is not enough can be taken from child play.  

Suppose a child, alone in a garden, picks up a different stone and says very serious, “I name you the name we will call the stone.”  

The child says the name with real trying to point.  

The child holds the stone and is seeing it.  

The child knows what giving a name is, because they have watched baby naming days and giving names to animals that live with people.  

Every part is met.  

Yet if no other person ever learns of or knows this name, the name has no good for other people, no power to make people follow, and does not stay beyond the child own mind.  

One can see that what the other cases share – and what the short saying does not need – is that the act of giving a name be put inside or accepted by a known people group and ideas that run the thing, so that the name has power to make people follow and can be shared, known, and kept by other people.
english_query: counterexample to: An act of naming succeeds in assigning a name N to an object O if and only if (i) a speaker utters N with the intention of designating O, (ii) the speaker is in perceptual contact with O at the time of the utterance, and (iii) the speaker has the concept of naming — that is, the speaker understands that the utterance is meant to establish a lasting designation.
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is that the rule about how a name works says a name works for a thing only when three things happen: the speaker says the name while trying to point to the thing, the speaker can see the thing at that moment, and the speaker knows that saying the name will make a label that stays. The second idea they say is that we can find a case where a name works even though one of those three things does not happen, and that this case shows the rule is not always right. 

In the case they give, a person hears a word for a thing in a story, later meets the real thing and uses the same word, even though at the time of the first saying the person was not looking at the real thing and was not trying to point to it, and the person did not think that the word would become a lasting label. Yet the word still ends up being used as a name for the thing. They say this fact is true: the name still works even without the three things the rule says are needed. 

From that fact they think we can follow that the rule is wrong, because they have a case where the rule says the name should not work but it does work. They think this means the rule does not cover all the ways a name can work, so the rule is too narrow. 

To check if the thinking is good, we look at whether the case really shows the rule fails. If the case truly shows a name working without any of the three things, then the rule does not hold in that situation and the rule would need to be changed. On the other hand, if the case still includes at least one of the three things in a different way – for example, the speaker may have had a mental picture of the thing or may have expected the word to stay as a label – then the rule might still be right and the case would not be a true counterexample. So the quality of the thinking depends on whether the case really lacks all three required parts. If it does, the thinking is good because it points out a real problem with the rule. If it does not, the thinking is weak because it rests on a mistaken view of what happened in the case.

question: counterexample to: A person A has an obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A has voluntarily undertaken to perform X, (ii) another person B has a legitimate expectation that A will perform X, and (iii) failure to perform X would cause harm to B.
expected_answer: One could say that a person must do a thing if he has taken it on by his own choice, another person has a true hope that he will do it, and not doing it would hurt that person. This shows the must that come from promise and promise work. A person who has agreed to make a house has taken the work by his own choice, the other person has a true hope that the house will be made, and not making it would hurt the other person. All three parts are met, and the must is clear. But a story that shows the not enough of this idea is the must to help. Think of a man walking by low water who sees a young child fall into water and start to go down. He has not made a promise to help the child. He does not know the child or the child family. He has not taken any help work by his own choice. No person has a true hope that he will help – the meeting is all by chance. All three parts of the idea do not work. But the must to go into the water and pull the child out is, for most good feeling, very strong. The man who walks by, leaving the child to go down because he has not taken it on by his own choice to save it, has not done a must that feels as big as any promise must. One could note that the idea has made the must look like it comes from own choice promise and has not seen the must that come just from the situation you are in – a must that the situation puts on us not because we chose it but because it is there, and that hold us even if we did not say yes to be held.
english_query: counterexample to: A person A has an obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) A has voluntarily undertaken to perform X, (ii) another person B has a legitimate expectation that A will perform X, and (iii) failure to perform X would cause harm to B.
ugf_response: The first idea they put forward is a rule that says a person A must do a thing X only when three things are true. The first of those three things is that A has said they will do X. The second thing is that another person B really hopes that A will do X. The third thing is that if A does not do X then B will be hurt. The second idea they give is an example that they say meets all three of those things but where A still does not have to do X. In their example A says they will do X, B really hopes A will do X, and B would be hurt if X is not done, yet A does not end up doing X. Because the example shows a case where the three conditions are there but the result the rule says should happen does not happen, they think the rule is not always true. The third idea is the conclusion that the rule is therefore false or at least not always correct. Whether this line of thought is good depends on whether the example really does have all three of the conditions. If the example truly has A’s own choice, B’s real hope, and B’s possible hurt, then it does show a case where the rule’s promise fails and the conclusion that the rule is not always right follows correctly. If, however, the example misses any of the three things – for instance if B’s hope is not strong enough to be called a real hope, or if A’s saying they will do X is not a true choice – then the example does not break the rule and the conclusion would be based on a mistake. So the reasoning is good only if the example truly satisfies each of the three parts; if it does, the thinking is sound and the rule is shown to be false in at least one case.

question: counterexample to: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T is grammatically well-formed in L2, and (iii) T employs vocabulary in L2 that corresponds to the vocabulary of S in L1.
expected_answer:
One person might say a good enough act of changing words from one language to another is a thing that keeps the true meaning, follows the ways that words are put together, and uses words that are the same as the first words.  

This idea shows the things we want: the right meaning, easy to read, and close enough.  

But a case that shows it is not enough comes from a well known thing of places where there are no words between languages.  

Think about a word from a language that many people in a part of the world speak that means a special kind of warm feeling, nice feeling, and a feeling of being at home.  

There is no one word in the language that many people in a part of the world speak that is the same as it.  

A good enough act of changing words from one language to another for a set of words that has that word has to use many words or a different way of saying – maybe “a warm and nice feeling” – but no one word in the language that many people in a part of the world speak is the same as that word.  

The one after the second way, which needs words that are the same, does not happen.  

Still the many word act of changing words from one language to another can be good enough: it keeps the true meaning, it follows the ways that words are put together, and it gives the idea of the first set of words with close enough.  

The idea, by saying we must have words that are the same, makes a part that cannot happen when languages cut the world in not the same ways with their words at the part.  

What we need is a less hard part – maybe that the act of changing words from one language to another just gives the idea of the first set of words using any words we have, without needing each word to have the same word.
english_query: counterexample to: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T is grammatically well-formed in L2, and (iii) T employs vocabulary in L2 that corresponds to the vocabulary of S in L1.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the case puts forward is that the three things they list are true about a good change of words from one language to another. They say that a good change of words must keep the meaning of the original, must follow the grammar rules of the new language, and must use words in the new language that match the words of the original.

The second idea they draw from that is that because those three things are true, a change of words that has them is automatically good, and that any good change of words must have them. In other words they think the three things are both needed and enough for a change of words to be good.

The third idea is the example they give to show that the rule does not work. In the example a change of words does keep the meaning, does follow the grammar rules, and does use matching words, but still feels wrong because it does not fit the way people normally speak or think in the new language. This shows that the three things are not enough to make a change of words good, and that a change of words can be good even if one of the three things is missing. Because the example breaks the link between the three things and the idea of a good change of words, the thinking that the three things guarantee a good change of words is not a good line of thought. The reasoning treats the three things as both necessary and sufficient without looking at other factors that matter, so the argument is not sound.

question: counterexample to: An event E constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) E involves the overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) E occurs rapidly relative to the normal pace of political change, and (iii) E results in a new political order that is qualitatively different from its predecessor.
expected_answer:
The big changes that many people call true big changes all have one thing that makes us want to look at them. The first big change in a country that started the idea that people have a right and that the people should decide was driven by the belief that people have a right and that the people should be the ones who decide. The second big change in a country that made clear ideas about the group that makes laws and about people being able to believe was shaped by those clear ideas. The third big change in a country that followed a clear belief in a right that comes from being a person and a shared idea that people agree to live together was led by that clear belief. In each case the big change was not just a take over of power but a change led by ideas about how the group that makes laws and the way people live should be set up.  

One could try to say that a big change is a quick take over of the old way a country runs that makes a new way that is very different. But a case that shows this short idea is not enough can be taken from the past of quick take overs. Imagine the leader of the fighters takes control in one week, breaks up the group that makes laws, stops the big set of laws for the country, and makes a way of deciding where the fighters are the only ones who decide. The old way is taken away. The change is quick. The new way – a way where one strong person who fights is the only one who decides – is very different from the old way where many people made laws together. All three parts are there. Yet some people see that this quick take over is not a big change. It does not have the belief part that made the first, second, and third big changes. The leader of the fighters has no plan for how people should live together, no clear idea of what is right, no want beyond making his own power stronger. What the past cases have together – and what the short idea does not ask for – is that the big change is led by a clear belief that moves the people and makes the new way they build.
english_query: counterexample to: An event E constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) E involves the overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) E occurs rapidly relative to the normal pace of political change, and (iii) E results in a new political order that is qualitatively different from its predecessor.
ugf_response:
The first idea they put forward is a short way of saying when we call something a big change we need three things: the event must take away the old way that runs a group, it must happen quick compared to the usual speed of group change, and it must make a new way that is not the same as the old way that was there before.  

The second idea they give is a real case that they say really happened. In this case a group of people changed their leader without any fight, the old way stayed the same, and the shift took a long time, but many people still call it a big change. The facts they point to are that the old way was not taken away, that the shift was not quick, and that the new way is mostly the same as the old way.  

The third idea they draw from the case is that because this event is called a big change even though it does not have all three of the things listed in the first idea, the short way of saying what a big change is must be wrong. In other words they think the three‑point rule does not match what people really call a big change, so the rule needs to be fixed or dropped.  

The fourth idea looks at whether that thinking is good. It is true that the case they point to does not fit the three‑point rule, so the rule is not able to cover every situation that people label a big change. That shows the rule is too narrow. However, the thinking also assumes that the way people use the word must include every single case, and it does not ask whether maybe the word is being used in a looser way for some events. So the criticism is useful because it points out a gap, but it may be a bit strong if the purpose of the rule was only to describe the most clear‑cut examples. In short, the case does show a problem with the rule, and the thought that the rule needs to be changed is reasonable, though one might still argue that the rule works for the kind of events it was meant to pick out.

question: counterexample to: A sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if (i) the truth of the proposition is taken for granted in the utterance, (ii) the negation of the sentence also takes the proposition for granted, and (iii) if the presupposed proposition is false, the sentence is neither true nor false.
expected_answer: Many people think that a piece of talk says an added idea when the truth of that idea is taken as true in the talk, and when the other piece of talk also takes it as true, and if the added idea is not true then the piece of talk is not true and not not true. The one after the second part gives the idea its strong point: it shows the added idea is not the same as a simple idea by putting it with places where there is no true or not true answer. But that part is not something that everyone agrees on. Look at the words “the person who leads the land we call has no hair on the head.” Both these words and the other words take it as true that the land we call has a person who leads; the first part and the second part are met. But if the land we call does not have a person who leads, is the piece of talk without a true or not true answer, or is it just not true? One well known idea says it is not true, calling the saying that there is a person who leads as not an added idea but as part of what the words say. In this way the one after the second part does not work, but we still want to say that the piece of talk takes it as true that there is a person who leads the land we call – the talk would be bad and have no point if no person who leads was. The meaning, it seems, has put together a real world thing – the parts for when a talk is good – with a meaning thing – the parts for when it has a true or not true answer – and these can be different.
english_query: counterexample to: A sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if (i) the truth of the proposition is taken for granted in the utterance, (ii) the negation of the sentence also takes the proposition for granted, and (iii) if the presupposed proposition is false, the sentence is neither true nor false.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is true is that the rule about when a sentence takes an idea for granted can be shown to be wrong by finding a sentence that fits the first two parts of the rule but does not fit the third part. They point to a specific sentence and say that in this sentence the truth of the idea is taken as true, and that even when the sentence is changed to use the not words, the idea is still taken as true. This matches the first two conditions of the rule. Then they note that the idea behind the sentence can be not true, and in that case the sentence still ends up being either true or false, not “neither true nor false” as the rule says it should. Because of this mismatch they claim the rule does not hold for all sentences.

The second idea they think follows from this is that if one can give just one such sentence, the whole rule must be rejected. They argue that the rule says the three parts must happen together for any sentence that takes an idea for granted, so a single example where the three parts do not happen together is enough to show the rule is not correct. Therefore they say the rule should be changed or dropped.

The third idea is about whether this way of thinking is good. It is good if the example really does meet the first two parts while failing the third part, because then the example does show a case where the rule does not work. If the example does not truly meet those conditions, or if the rule was meant to apply only in a more limited situation, then the reasoning would be weaker. In general, using one clear example to show a rule can be wrong is a solid way to argue, as long as the example is described correctly and fits the conditions that the rule talks about.

question: counterexample to: A political arrangement P is legitimate if and only if (i) P derives its authority from the consent of the governed, (ii) the consent is given freely and without coercion, and (iii) the governed have a genuine opportunity to withdraw their consent if the arrangement fails to serve their interests.
expected_answer:
The main cases of just control that come from clear yes of the people who live under it. In a town meeting in the new part of the country, people came together to talk and to say yes to the things that tell them how to live. The early written paper was a note that people signed by their own will, making them follow the same set of things that tell people how to live. The big meeting in 1787 made a set of things that people talked about, wrote down, and all said yes to by people chosen to speak for the people. In each other case, the people group got its control from the real clear yes of the people who would live under it.

Someone could try to write down these ideas by saying a just people group thing is one that gets its control from the yes of the people and that lets people stop saying yes when the thing does not help what they want. This idea shows the town meeting, the paper, and the big meeting. But a case that shows it is not enough comes when we think about a kind thing from another land that was taken by strong hand and never said yes by the people, but that gives no war, just law, good things, and safe life. The people have no way to stop saying yes, because they never said yes. All three parts of the idea do not work. Yet imagine the people, asked in a very true way, say they feel good with the thing and do not want it to change.

Someone could see that the thing gives real and liked good things, and that to say it is not just at all seems to not see the good of the very people whose wants the idea says it will keep safe. The other cases, put together with the other land case, show a different in the idea of just control. The idea shows just by yes but cannot fit just by good — and the other land case says the two may be not together. One thing may be said yes to without giving good things to the people, and it may give good things to the people without being said yes to. The idea, by making yes the only place for just, cannot fit this different.
english_query: counterexample to: A political arrangement P is legitimate if and only if (i) P derives its authority from the consent of the governed, (ii) the consent is given freely and without coercion, and (iii) the governed have a genuine opportunity to withdraw their consent if the arrangement fails to serve their interests.
ugf_response:
The first idea in the case is a rule about when a system is called right. The rule says that a system is right if it gets its power from the free agreement of the people, if that agreement is given without any force, and if the people have a real chance to stop the agreement when the system does not work for their needs. The person who makes the case first says that this rule is not enough, and they give an example that they think shows the rule does not work.

The next thing they say is that their example fits the three parts of the rule. They tell about a system that was started because the people agreed to it, that the agreement was made without force, and that the people could stop the system if it stopped meeting their needs. Yet, in their story, the system still does things that many people think are not right. Because the example matches the three parts, they say the rule must be wrong.

The last thing they think follows from that is that the rule cannot be used to decide when a system is right. Since they have an example that meets the three parts but still seems not right, they conclude that the rule does not capture what we need to call a system right.

Whether this thinking is good depends on whether the example truly meets all three parts. If the people really had a real chance to stop the system, and the agreement was truly free and without force, then the example does show a problem for the rule. But often a chance to stop is only real if the people can actually make the system change, and a free agreement may be missing if the people feel pressure or lack other options. If the example hides any of those hidden pressures or limits, then the claim that it fits the three parts is weak. In that case the thinking would not be strong, because the example would not really be a fair test of the rule. If, however, the example truly has a free agreement, no force, and a real chance to stop, then the thinking is good: it points out that the rule alone may not be enough to tell when a system is right.

question: counterexample to: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B are composed of the same material parts, (ii) A and B have the same form or structure — that is, their parts are arranged in the same way, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 with B at t2 along which no disruption of existence occurs.
expected_answer:
One person might say that a thing at one time is the same as a thing at another time only if the two are made of the same stuff parts, they have the same shape, and there is an always time and place way that joins them.  

The idea shows some easy case. A piece of rock taken from a hill and put in a field is, after 20 years, the same piece of rock if it is made of the same stone, keeps the same shape, and has stayed in its place all the time.  

The being the same of simple not changing things seems to sit on just these three things.  

But we have to think about a human. A man at 60 is the same man who at 20 blew out the light on his day sweet food. His friends know him; the law says he must keep promises made many years before; he remembers things from his young days as things that happened to him.  

But the stuff that makes his body has been almost all changed in the 40 years between. The small pieces that made his bones at 20 are not the small pieces that make them at 60; the small parts of his skin, his blood, his inside parts have been made new many times by the usual ways that life keeps them working.  

People who study how bodies work think that in about 7 to 10 years the stuff of a human body is almost all changed.  

The first thing of the idea does not work: the man at 60 is not made of the same stuff parts as the man at 20. But his being the same through time is not in big question.  

The idea has used the staying the same of stuff make up – which is enough for the being the same of not alive things like rocks – and said it is a needed thing for all being the same through time, missing the thing that living things stay the same by changing their stuff parts in an always way of making new and fixing.
english_query: counterexample to: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B are composed of the same material parts, (ii) A and B have the same form or structure — that is, their parts are arranged in the same way, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 with B at t2 along which no disruption of existence occurs.
ugf_response:
The first idea they say is true is that a rule says a thing at one time is the same as a thing at another time only when three things are true. Those three things are: the two things are made of the same stuff, the two things have the same shape, and there is a line of places and times that goes without a break where the thing keeps being there. They then give a case of a chair that loses a leg and gets a new leg. They claim that in that case the chair at the later time still uses the same stuff, still has the same shape, and still has a line of places and times without a break, so all three of the rule’s requirements are met.

The second idea they think follows is that because the three requirements are met, the rule says the chair at the later time must be the same as the chair at the earlier time. Since many people feel that a chair that has a new leg is not the same chair, they say this shows the rule gives a wrong answer and therefore the rule itself must be false. In other words they think the case shows the rule does not work.

The thinking can be checked by looking at each of the three requirements. The first requirement, that the two chairs are made of the same stuff, is not true because the new leg is made of different stuff from the old leg that was taken away. The second requirement, that the two chairs have the same shape, is also not true because the shape changes when a leg is missing and then when a new leg is put on. The third requirement, that there is a line of places and times without a break where the chair keeps being there, is not true either because there is a break when the old leg is taken away and the chair is not whole for a short time. Because at least one of the three things is not true, the rule does not say the two chairs are the same, so the step from the three things to the claim that the rule is broken does not follow. The reasoning therefore is not good; the case does not succeed as a counterexample to the rule.

question: counterexample to: Two or more agents cooperate if and only if (i) each agent is aware that they are participating in a joint activity, (ii) each agent intentionally contributes to a shared goal, and (iii) the agents have communicated with one another about their respective roles.
expected_answer:
The people who help each other with getting the food from the ground, the people who make music with four people together, the people who study how things work and write a piece of writing — these are clear things of people working together. In each thing, the people know they are doing a thing together, each does a thing for the same thing they want, and the people have talked about who does what. From these things one can make a simple thought: people work together if and only if each knows they are doing a thing together, each does a thing for the same thing they want, and the people have talked about who does what.  

But this thought, taken from things where the work together was made and talked about, leaves out a big area of work together that happens on its own and without talk. Think of two people who drive cars toward the one after the first on a small way. Each go slow and turn left. Not one has talked to the one after the first; no talk, no hand move, no sign go between them except the move of the cars themselves. Yet each knows they are doing a thing together – the trying to get past the one after the first in a small place – and each does a thing for the same thing they want to get past without a hit. The first two parts are true. But the one after the two parts is not: no talk about who does what has happened. The people who drive use a same thought – the way cars go – and they watch each other move. Their work together is on its own and without words and works well.  

The other things all had made work together where who does what was talked about and made, and the simple thought said that talk was must. But what made those things work together was not the talk itself but the way each people watch and answer the one after the first while both try for the same thing. The people who drive on the small way show just this watching and answering. The thought put together a same way of work together talking about who does what with something that must always be there and did not see the work together that happens without talk by use the same thought and the quick watch and answer of people to the one after the first things.
english_query: counterexample to: Two or more agents cooperate if and only if (i) each agent is aware that they are participating in a joint activity, (ii) each agent intentionally contributes to a shared goal, and (iii) the agents have communicated with one another about their respective roles.
ugf_response:
The first idea in the case is a claim that people work together only when three things happen: first, each person knows they are part of a together work; second, each person does something on purpose for the same aim; and third, the people have talked with each other about what each will do. The claim says that if any one of those three things is missing, then the people will not work together, and that if all three are present, they will work together.

The second idea is a story that tries to show the claim is not true. In the story, one person lifts a box and another person pushes the box from behind. Both people want the box to move, both know they are part of a together work, and both act on purpose for the same aim, but they never talk about who will lift and who will push. Even without that talk, they still move the box together. The story says that this shows a situation where the first two things are there, the third thing is not there, and yet the people still work together, so the claim that all three are needed is false.

The third idea looks at whether the thinking that the story disproves the claim is good. If the story really has no talk at all, then the step from the story to the claim is a correct step: a single example where the third thing is missing but the work still happens does show that the claim is not always right. The thinking is good as long as the story truly lacks any form of talk, including signs or gestures that could count as communication. If one can argue that the people gave each other signs, then the story might still have a kind of talk, and the claim would not be fully broken. So the quality of the thinking depends on how strictly we define “talk,” but the basic move—using a case where one of the three parts is missing to challenge a rule that says all three are required—is a sound way to test the rule.

question: counterexample to: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory — no higher authority overrides its decisions, (ii) E controls the use of force within that territory, and (iii) E is recognised as sovereign by other sovereign entities.
expected_answer:
Someone could say that a group that makes laws for a place is the top group only if it has the final say inside a set land, keeps control of the use of force inside that land, and is said to be the top group by other groups that are also top.  

The first part shows the inside side of being the top: inside its land, the word of the top group is final.  

The second part makes sure that the top group has the ways to make its word be followed.  

The third part adds an outside side: being the top is not just an inside thing but also needs people in many places to say it is true.  

All together, the parts show a group that makes laws for a place that is the most inside, can keep safe itself, and is said to be the top by people outside.  

But someone could see that the part about people saying it is the top brings a strange circle and a hard outside side.  

Think about the place that many people talk about.  

That place has a working group that makes laws and has the final say inside its land.  

It has a group of soldiers and a group of people who keep order that keep control of the use of force inside its edges.  

It makes its own outside plan, makes its own money, and runs its own laws.  

By the first and second parts, that place looks like it is the top.  

But many other places do not say it is the top, mostly because they want to stay nice with the big country that says it owns the land.  

If the law is used exactly, that place is not the top — not because it does not have any real part of being the top, but because some outside groups think it is easier to say it is not.  

The law, it seems, has put the idea of being the top in part in the hands of outside people whose thoughts may show a hard choice instead of any look at how the group really works.
english_query: counterexample to: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory — no higher authority overrides its decisions, (ii) E controls the use of force within that territory, and (iii) E is recognised as sovereign by other sovereign entities.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the case puts forward is that there is a group that makes the top say for a place, that can make people use power in that place, and that other groups that also make the top say think that this group also makes the top say. The case says that all three of those things happen for the group, and that the group still is not treated as the one with the top say. This part of the case is trying to show a fact that the three things can happen without the group being seen as the one with the top say.

The second idea that follows from that fact is that the rule that says a group is the one with the top say only when it does those three things cannot be right. Because the case shows a group that does the three things but is still not the one with the top say, the case says the rule must be wrong. The thinking is that one example that does not fit a rule means the rule does not work for all groups.

The third idea is an evaluation of that thinking. The thinking is good if the example really does have the three things and still is not seen as the one with the top say. If that is true, then the rule does miss a case and needs to be changed or added to. If, however, the example is not a true case of a group that does all three things, or if the way other groups think about it is not the same as the rule expects, then the thinking may be missing something. In general, pointing out a case that does not fit a rule is a useful way to test the rule, but the test only works when the case is described correctly and the rule is meant to cover all such cases. So the case makes a clear point that the rule may be too narrow, and the reasoning is solid as long as the facts of the case are correct.

question: counterexample to: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) C groups objects that share important properties, (ii) the groupings in C are stable under new observations — that is, newly discovered objects can be assigned to existing categories without restructuring the system, and (iii) C is consistent with the best available scientific theory.
expected_answer:
The best ways that people have put things into groups — a set of many small parts that are put in order by the way their inside parts are built, a way that a well known old person put living things into groups by the way their bodies look, a way that people put rocks into groups by the way their tiny parts are built — all have a same good point.  

Each one puts things together by parts about them that you cannot just see on the outside but that are part of the thing itself and that make other parts about the thing that people who study care about.  

The first set puts many small parts together by parts that come from the way their inside parts are built; the second way puts living things together by the way their bodies look, which shows they share a same family; the rock way uses the way the tiny parts are built, which decides how hard they are, how they break, and how they let light go through.  

From these big case we can try to make a way to put things into groups that is good for study only when it puts together things that share big important parts, when the groups stay the same when we see new things, and when the whole way fits with the best ideas we have.  

But this way, even though it works for the big case, also lets in ways to put things together that are not good for study.  

Think about a way to put stars into groups by how bright they look from our world — how much light we see.  

Stars that look about the same bright share a part that matters when we look at them; new stars we find can be given a bright look number without changing the groups; and this way of grouping fits well with the ideas we have about space, because the bright look number is a clear thing we can see.  

All three parts of the way are true.  

But this way of grouping does not tell us much about the stars, because it puts together stars that are really very different — a small close star that is not bright and a huge far star that is very bright can look the same bright — and it puts together stars that are really the same.  

The part we use to make the groups — how bright they look — is about where we are looking from, not about any real part of the stars.  

The big case worked because it used parts that belong to the things themselves, not because of where we look at them.  

The way says the parts we group by must be important, but it does not say they must be parts that belong to the things themselves and not to the way we see them.
english_query: counterexample to: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) C groups objects that share important properties, (ii) the groupings in C are stable under new observations — that is, newly discovered objects can be assigned to existing categories without restructuring the system, and (iii) C is consistent with the best available scientific theory.
ugf_response:
The first idea that the person says is true is that a way to put things into groups can be called good for study when it puts together things that have important things in common. The second idea that they say is true is that the groups should not have to be changed a lot when new things are found – new things can be placed into the groups that already exist without having to rebuild the whole set of groups. The third idea that they say is true is that the way of grouping should fit with the best big idea that science has right now. They then say that a way to put things into groups is good for study if and only if all three of those ideas are true at the same time.

The person then gives a case that they say shows the rule does not work. In this case there is a way to put things into groups that does put together things that share important things, and it also lets new things be added without having to change the existing groups. However, this way does not fit with the best big idea that science has right now. Because the case meets the first two ideas but not the third, the person says the rule is broken and therefore the rule is not correct. This is the fourth idea that they think follows from the case – that a single example that fails one of the three ideas is enough to show the whole “if and only if” claim is wrong.

To see whether the thinking is good we have to look at the link between the case and the rule. The claim that the three ideas together are needed for a good way of grouping is a strong claim – it says that missing any one of them makes the whole thing not good. If that claim is true, then a case that misses one of them would indeed show the claim is false. The problem is that the claim may not be true in the first place; it might be that a way of grouping can still be useful for study even if it does not fit the current best big idea, or that the “if and only if” wording is too strict. The reasoning also assumes that the case really does meet the first two ideas perfectly, which may be open to question. If the case does not fully meet those ideas, then it does not break the rule. So the thinking is only as good as the truth of the three ideas and the accuracy of the case. If either of those parts is weak, the conclusion that the rule is wrong does not hold strong.

question: counterexample to: A painting represents an object if and only if it visually resembles that object in relevant respects, competent viewers recognise the object in the painting, and the painter intended to depict that object.
expected_answer:
If a person looks at many pictures that clearly show what they are about — a picture of a rich man, a picture of a place with land and water, a picture of food that grows on a living thing — that person may think that a picture needs to look like the thing, that a person who looks can see the thing, and that the person who paints meant to show the thing. In each case, the shapes on the paper are the same as the parts of the thing that can be seen, a person who looks can see the thing easy, and the person who paints tried to show it. The big idea that this is the clear idea for what a picture is comes easy: these three parts hold what the cases have the same.

But the big idea is too small when we try to use it on other pictures. Imagine a person sees a picture of a sound thing with thin lines that make sound that is made in a kind that breaks the thing into many flat shapes. The paper shows many flat shapes that cover each other and show many views at the same time, and it does not look like a sound thing in the simple way we usually see a sound thing. A person who looks can not see the usual shape, the dark color of a tree, or the thin lines that make sound just by looking. The first part of the big idea is not met. Still, a person who looks who knows the usual way of this kind can see a sound thing in the picture. They know that the broken look is a way to show a thing, not a problem that stops the picture from showing it. The person who paints also meant to show a sound thing. The second and third parts are met. So the picture can be a picture of a sound thing in a good way. This makes us see that the idea we made from pictures that look like real things was wrong to think that looking like the thing is always needed. A picture can show a thing by using a usual way, by making a shape, and by learned power, even when it does not look like the thing in the way the idea says.
english_query: counterexample to: A painting represents an object if and only if it visually resembles that object in relevant respects, competent viewers recognise the object in the painting, and the painter intended to depict that object.
ugf_response:
The first idea they talk about is a rule that says a picture shows a thing when three things are true: the picture looks like that thing in the right ways, people who know can see the thing in the picture, and the person who makes the picture wanted to show that thing. The second idea they bring up is a special picture that they think makes the rule not work. They say this picture looks like a certain thing, that people who know can see that thing in the picture, and that the person who made the picture did not want to show that thing. Because the picture still seems to show the thing even though the third part of the rule is not true, they say this is an example that shows the rule is wrong.

The third idea they draw from this is that because they have found a picture that meets the first two parts of the rule but not the third, the rule cannot be right. They think that finding even one picture like this is enough to stop the rule from being true for all pictures.

To see if the thinking is good we can look at each part. The first part of the rule – that the picture looks like the thing in the right ways – is true for the picture they give. The second part – that people who know can see the thing in the picture – also seems true, because people who understand the picture can point out the thing. The third part – that the maker wanted to show the thing – is the part they claim is false. If it is really false, then the picture still makes people think of the thing, which means the rule says the picture should not count as showing the thing. The example therefore shows a case where the rule says “no” but our ordinary feeling says “yes”. That does make the rule seem too strict.

The reasoning that one such case is enough to say the whole rule is wrong is a normal way to test a rule. If a rule says “all must have this three parts” and we find even one case that has the first two parts but not the third, the rule cannot be true for every case. So the move from the example to the claim that the rule fails is a good move. The only thing that could weaken the thinking is if the picture they use does not really have the first two parts, or if the maker actually did want to show the thing in some hidden way. If those doubts are cleared, then the example does indeed show that the rule does not match how we think pictures work. In that situation the thinking is solid and the rule would need to be changed.

question: counterexample to: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A cites antecedent conditions necessary for E, (ii) A subsumes E under a general regularity or pattern, and (iii) A is consistent with the surviving documentary evidence.
expected_answer:
The best clear stories about the past all have a big part that we can look at. When the first writer told about the big war in an old place, he said there was a big reason — the rise of a city and the fear it made — and also the small reasons that started the fighting. When the writer who told about a big change in 1789 told the story, he put the things that happened in 1789 inside a long change of the people there, but he did not say they were only a simple idea. When the writer who told about the fall of a sea area told the story, he put together the edges of the land, the ways money moved, and the small things run by the people who run the place over many times. In each story, the telling worked because it put together the big picture and the small picture — by showing how big reasons made the small things happen but did not decide all.

Someone could try to make a simple idea for a good story about the past by saying it must name the things that had to be there first, put the thing inside a big way, and fit what the old papers say. But a story about the fall of a big city in 1453 shows that this simple idea is not enough. We can name the things that had to be there — the old group of people who fight got less strong, a new group got bigger, and help from the west did not come — and we can put the fall inside the big way that big groups whose groups of people who fight get less strong while a close group gets bigger can be taken over. The story fits all the old papers. All three things are there. But the story does not tell why the fall happened in the way it did: the leader used many big things that can hurt that had never been used before, a lucky find of a door that was left open, and small choices of the people who tried to keep safe. These small things were needed for how the fall really was. Someone can see that other stories show, and the simple idea does not get, that a story about the past must put together the big picture and the small picture, by putting big form reasons to the small things that make each past event have its own shape.
english_query: counterexample to: An account A constitutes a satisfactory historical explanation of an event E if and only if (i) A cites antecedent conditions necessary for E, (ii) A subsumes E under a general regularity or pattern, and (iii) A is consistent with the surviving documentary evidence.
ugf_response:
First, we see that the person tells a story about a past thing and says the story does three things. The first idea says the story must name things that happened before the thing. The second idea says the story must put the thing inside a way that often repeats. The third idea says the story must fit the papers we still have. The person then shows a case that the story does all three things but still does not give people a clear picture of why the thing happened. This case is used to show that the three ideas may not always work.

Second, they think that because this case shows a story can do all three things and still not be a good story, the three ideas cannot be enough to make a good story. So they think the main idea is not true. They also think we need some other thing, like a check on how well the story explains the cause, to make a good story.

Third, whether this thinking is good depends on if the case really shows a problem. If the story in the case still leaves out an important part, like why the thing happened, then it is fair to say the three ideas are not enough. On the other hand, if the story actually does give a good reason but we just do not see it, then the case might not be a real problem. In general, pointing out a case that seems to break an idea is a useful way to look at the idea. It helps us see if we need to add more ideas, like looking at how well the story fits with what we know about why things happen.
